figures.name: Ibrahim ibn al-Aghlab
figures.title: Founder of the Aghlabid Dynasty
figures.roleDescription: First Aghlabid ruler who established the dynasty in Ifriqiya
figures.significance: Founded the Aghlabid dynasty and established hereditary rule over Ifriqiya under Abbasid suzerainty.
figures.biography:
Ibrahim ibn al-Aghlab was the son of al-Aghlab ibn Salim, a provincial leader who had served as governor of Ifriqiya under the Muhallabids. When the Muhallabid rule came undone due to a minor rebellion in Tunis that spread to Kairouan, Ibrahim led a disciplined army and managed to reestablish stability in 797. Recognizing an opportunity, he proposed to the Abbasid caliph Harun al-Rashid that he be granted Ifriqiya as a hereditary fief with the title of amir, to which the caliph acquiesced in 800.

Although the Abbasid caliphs continued to receive an annual tribute and their suzerainty was referenced in the Friday prayers, Ibrahim's control was largely symbolic. His reign from 800-812 marked the beginning of the Aghlabid dynasty that would rule Ifriqiya for over a century. The Aghlabids were predominantly of the Arab Bani Tamim tribe, and Ibrahim established a system that provided political peace and stability following a period of instability, leading to economic expansion and prosperity including admired public construction projects.
achievements: Founded the Aghlabid dynasty
Established hereditary rule over Ifriqiya
Negotiated autonomy from Abbasid caliphate
Restored stability to the region

figures.name: Abu Muhammad Ziyadat Allah I ibn Ibrahim ibn al-Aghlab
figures.title: Aghlabid Amir of Ifriqiya
figures.roleDescription: Third Aghlabid ruler who initiated the conquest of Sicily
figures.significance: Launched the Muslim conquest of Sicily and established more stable Aghlabid control over Ifriqiya through alliance with Berbers.
figures.biography:
Ziyadat Allah I was the son of Ibrahim ibn al-Aghlab, the founder of the Aghlabid dynasty. He ruled as amir of Ifriqiya from 817 until his death in 838, and his reign marked a shift towards greater control and stability for the emirs in Ifriqiya. Despite being an independent ruler, Ziyadat Allah faced the largest jund revolt in Ifriqiya's history, which broke out in 824 and saw most of the region conquered by the rebelling Arab military forces. Unlike previous rulers who had to deal with jund and Berber problems simultaneously, Ziyadat Allah had established a working relationship with the Berbers.

With this Berber alliance, he was able to employ Berber forces to defeat the rebelling jund, which allowed him not only to avoid losing control but to turn Aghlabid energies towards conquest. In 831, Ziyadat Allah launched an invasion of Sicily, placing the qadi Asad ibn al-Furat in command and terming the military adventure a jihad. This expedition proved successful, with Palermo being made the capital of the region captured. The conquest of Sicily marked a new era for the Aghlabids and is why Ziyadat is sometimes described as the second founder of the Aghlabid regime, having solved the jund and Berber issues that had faced all preceding emirs.
achievements: Launched the Muslim conquest of Sicily
Defeated the largest jund revolt in Ifriqiya
Established stable alliance with Berber tribes
Built ribats at Monastir and Susa

figures.name: Abu Ishaq Ibrahim II ibn Ahmad
figures.title: Emir of Ifriqiya
figures.roleDescription: Aghlabid ruler known for his military campaigns and architectural projects
figures.significance: Completed the conquest of Sicily and built the palace-city of Raqqada, but gained a reputation for cruelty and tyranny.
figures.biography:
Ibrahim II was the son of the Aghlabid emir Ahmad of Ifriqiya and ruled from 875 until his abdication in 902. During his brother Muhammad II's frivolous reign, Ibrahim served as governor of Kairouan with exemplary efficiency, earning him much admiration. When Muhammad II died in 875, Ibrahim's candidacy was supported by popular crowds and endorsed by the jurists of Kairouan. Among his major public works, Ibrahim completed construction of the Zaytuna mosque of Tunis, enlarged the Uqba mosque of Kairouan, built a vast new water reservoir for the city, erected the walls of Sousse, and established naval signal towers along the Ifriqiyan coast. Most notably, in 876 he erected the new palace-city of Raqqada just southwest of Kairouan, built on a grandiose scale with walls ten kilometers long.

As his reign progressed, Ibrahim acquired a reputation for tyranny and homicidal sadism, with chronicles reporting that he took great pleasure in cruelty and killing, often conducting executions personally. He was a centralizing ruler who mistrusted the old Arab aristocracy and sought to undermine the semi-autonomous Arab regiments by expanding his Sudanese slave-soldier regiments to as much as 10,000 men. Under Ibrahim's rule, most of Sicily was completed in Muslim hands - Syracuse fell in 878, and after his final campaign, Taormina fell in 902, completing the 75-year conquest. However, mounting reports of his cruel atrocities prompted the Abbasid Caliph al-Mu'tadid to recall him to Baghdad in 901-902, leading to his abdication and final campaign where he died of dysentery near Cosenza in 902.
achievements: Completed the conquest of Sicily
Built the palace-city of Raqqada
Completed major mosque and infrastructure projects
Established extensive naval signal tower system

figures.name: Ziyadat Allah III
figures.title: Last Aghlabid Emir
figures.roleDescription: Final ruler of the Aghlabid dynasty, overthrown by the Fatimids
figures.significance: Last ruler of the Aghlabid dynasty, whose weakness led to the Fatimid conquest of Ifriqiya in 909.
figures.biography:
Ziyadat Allah III was the eleventh and final Aghlabid amir, ruling from 902-909. He came to power after his father Ibrahim II's death during the siege of Cosenza in 902. Due to insecurity stemming from his father's assassination and the dynasty's internal conflicts, Ziyadat Allah III ordered the execution of his rival brothers and uncles, which significantly weakened the dynasty during a critical period when the Fatimids were mounting their assault against Aghlabid domains.

His reign coincided with the rise of the Fatimid movement in the far west of Ifriqiya, led by Abu Abdallah al-Shi'i among the Kutama Berbers. The Fatimids launched frequent attacks that provoked general unrest and increased Aghlabid political instability. The weakened state, crippled by Ibrahim II's destruction of the Arab aristocracy and the departure of much of the Ifriqiyan army for the Italian campaign, proved unable to resist the Kutama advance. The Fatimids eventually managed to capture Kairouan in 909, forcing Ziyadat Allah III to evacuate his palace at Raqqada and flee, marking the end of the Aghlabid dynasty after over a century of rule.

figures.name: Abu Abdallah al-Shi'i
figures.title: Fatimid Da'i and Military Leader
figures.roleDescription: Ismaili missionary who conquered the Aghlabid state for the Fatimids
figures.significance: Led the Kutama Berber forces that conquered Aghlabid Ifriqiya and established Fatimid rule in 909.
figures.biography:
Abu Abdallah al-Shi'i was an Ismaili da'i (missionary) originally from San'a in Yemen who came to the Maghreb to propagate Ismaili beliefs. In 893, he first settled among the Banu Saktan tribe, part of the larger Kutama tribe, in Ikjan near the city of Mila in northeastern Algeria. Due to hostility from local Aghlabid authorities and other Kutama tribes, he was forced to seek protection from the Banu Ghashman tribe in Tazrut, from where he began building support for the Fatimid movement.

Abu Abdallah established an Isma'ili theocratic state based in Tazrut and gradually won over most Kutama tribes through persuasion or coercion. Over the following years, he led increasingly successful military campaigns against the Aghlabids, capturing key cities including Mila, Setif, Tubna, and Baghaya. On 25 February 909, he set out from Ikjan with an army of 200,000 men for the final invasion of Kairouan. After defeating the last Aghlabid forces near al-Aribus on 18 March, he entered Raqqada on 25 March 909, effectively ending Aghlabid rule. He then led his army west to retrieve Abdallah al-Mahdi from Sijilmasa, who was proclaimed the first Fatimid caliph in 910, though Abu Abdallah was later killed in a dispute with his former protégé.
achievements: Conquered Aghlabid Ifriqiya for the Fatimids
Organized and led Kutama Berber forces
Established Ismaili theocratic state
Ended over a century of Aghlabid rule

figures.name: Sultan Saeed Al Darmaki
figures.title: Film Director
figures.roleDescription: Emirati film director
figures.significance: First Emirati director to direct an American fantasy comedy film released internationally.
figures.biography:
Sultan Saeed Al Darmaki is an Emirati film director who made his directorial debut with the 2015 American fantasy comedy film 'Kids vs Monsters.' The film was released on video on demand on September 29, 2015, marking a significant milestone for Emirati cinema in international markets.

The film featured a notable cast including Malcolm McDowell, Lance Henriksen, Keith David, and Elaine Hendrix. The story centered around six children sent to a mysterious house by their wealthy parents, where they face seven monsters intent on battling them. Despite mixed reviews, with some critics calling it 'tedious' while others found it engaging due to its cast and unique approach, Al Darmaki's work represents an important step in Emirati filmmaking reaching global audiences.
achievements: Directed the 2015 film 'Kids vs Monsters'
Made directorial debut with international cast

figures.name: Awad Al Darmaki
figures.title: Writer
figures.roleDescription: Emirati writer and author
figures.significance: Notable Emirati writer mentioned alongside other prominent UAE literary figures.
figures.biography:
Awad Al Darmaki is an Emirati writer who has contributed to the literary landscape of the United Arab Emirates. He is mentioned among notable Emirati authors and writers, indicating his recognition within the country's cultural and literary circles.

Al Darmaki's work is part of the broader movement of Emirati literature that has emerged alongside the development of the UAE as a nation. He represents the Al Darmaki family's contribution to the intellectual and cultural development of the Emirates, moving beyond traditional roles into modern literary expression.

figures.name: Rayan Yaslam Al Jaberi
figures.title: Footballer
figures.roleDescription: Emirati professional footballer
figures.significance: Professional footballer representing the Al Jaberi family in UAE sports.
figures.biography: Rayan Yaslam Al Jaberi is an Emirati footballer who is part of the Al Jaberi family, which is a subsection of the Dhawahir tribe. The Dhawahir tribe has its main center in the Buraimi Oasis and Al Ain, with long-standing alliances with the ruling Al Nahyan family of Abu Dhabi. The Al Jaberi family is one of the notable branches within this tribal confederation that has historically been influential in the UAE region.

figures.name: Rami Yaslam Al Jaberi
figures.title: Footballer
figures.roleDescription: Emirati professional footballer
figures.significance: Professional footballer representing the Al Jaberi family in UAE sports.
figures.biography: Rami Yaslam Al Jaberi is an Emirati footballer who is part of the Al Jaberi family, which belongs to the Dhawahir tribe. The Dhawahir tribe has been historically significant in the UAE, particularly in the Al Ain and Buraimi Oasis regions. The family has maintained close relationships with the ruling families of the UAE and has been part of the broader tribal confederation that helped shape the modern Emirates.

figures.name: Ali bin Swelah Al Jaberi
figures.title: Actor and Comedian
figures.roleDescription: Emirati entertainment personality
figures.significance: Notable Emirati actor and comedian representing the Al Jaberi family in the entertainment industry.
figures.biography: Ali bin Swelah Al Jaberi is an Emirati actor and comedian who has made his mark in the UAE entertainment industry. As a member of the Al Jaberi family, he represents one of the notable branches of the Dhawahir tribe, which has historically been influential in the Al Ain and Buraimi Oasis regions. His work in comedy and acting contributes to the cultural landscape of the UAE while maintaining his family's presence in various sectors of Emirati society.

figures.name: Waleed Salem Sulaiman Al Jaberi
figures.title: Footballer
figures.roleDescription: Emirati professional footballer
figures.significance: Professional footballer contributing to UAE sports while representing the Al Jaberi family.
figures.biography: Waleed Salem Sulaiman Al Jaberi is an Emirati footballer who is part of the Al Jaberi family within the Dhawahir tribal confederation. The Al Jaberi family has been part of the historical fabric of the UAE, particularly in the regions around Al Ain and Buraimi Oasis. His involvement in professional football represents the family's continued participation in various aspects of modern Emirati society, from sports to entertainment and other professional fields.

figures.name: Muhammed Reza al-Jaberi
figures.title: Chief of Protocol and Public Relations
figures.roleDescription: Iraqi PMF official
figures.significance: Iraqi Popular Mobilization Forces official who was killed alongside Qasem Soleimani in the 2020 Baghdad airport strike.
figures.biography: Muhammed Reza al-Jaberi served as the chief of protocol and public relations for Iraq's Popular Mobilization Forces (PMF). He was among the ten people killed in the U.S. drone strike on January 3, 2020, at Baghdad International Airport that targeted Iranian General Qasem Soleimani. Al-Jaberi was traveling with Soleimani and Abu Mahdi al-Muhandis, the deputy chairman of the PMF, when their convoy was struck by missiles from an MQ-9 Reaper drone as they departed the airport. The strike was ordered by U.S. President Donald Trump and was intended to prevent what American officials claimed were imminent attacks on U.S. personnel. Al-Jaberi's death was part of a broader escalation in U.S.-Iran tensions that brought the region close to open conflict.

figures.name: Muhammad Saleh Al-Jaberi
figures.title: Scholar and Writer
figures.roleDescription: Tunisian researcher, novelist, and cultural administrator
figures.significance: Prominent Tunisian scholar who specialized in Maghreb literature and served as a cultural administrator with ALECSO.
figures.biography:
Muhammad Saleh Al-Jaberi was born in Tozeur, Tunisia in February 1940 and died in Ariana, Tunisia in June 2009. He specialized in recording the historical and literary movement in the Maghreb, particularly in Algeria, Libya, and Tunisia, making significant contributions to the study of Tunisian literature and culture. Al-Jaberi received his early education in Tozeur, attending a Kuttab and then Ibn-Shubbaṭ elementary school until 1953, before completing his secondary studies at the Secondary Institution in Tozeur. He continued his education at Zaytuna University in Tunis, obtaining a certificate in 1961, and later studied in Baghdad where he obtained a licence. He earned a Master's of Advanced Study in Algeria in 1980 and a Doctorate degree specializing in Bayram al-Tunisi from the University of Algeria.

Throughout his career, Al-Jaberi held various educational and cultural positions, including teacher in Al-Wardiya District in Tunis, high school teacher in Monastir, director of literature in the Ministry of Culture, and director of the Tunisian Cultural Center in Tripoli. In 1979, he became a member of the Arab League Educational, Cultural and Scientific Organization (ALECSO), where he supervised the cultural department until his retirement in 2000. After retirement, he was appointed head of an encyclopedic project about notable Arab and Muslim figures and scholars, with nineteen volumes published during his oversight.
achievements: Authored multiple novels including 'Laylatul Sanawatil ʿAshr' which was chosen among the 100 best Arabic novels
Supervised cultural department at ALECSO for over 20 years
Led encyclopedic project on notable Arab and Muslim figures with 19 published volumes
Wrote extensively on Maghreb literature and culture

figures.name: Sheikh Rashid bin Majid Al Mualla
figures.title: Ruler of Umm Al Quwain
figures.roleDescription: First ruler of Umm Al Quwain (1768-1820)
figures.significance: Founded the Al Mualla dynasty and established Umm Al Quwain as an independent sheikhdom.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Rashid bin Majid Al Mualla was the first known head of the Al Ali tribe when they settled at Umm Al Quwain and the founder of the Al Mualla dynasty. He ruled from 1768 to 1820, establishing the foundations of what would become the Emirate of Umm Al Quwain.

Sheikh Rashid was responsible for the construction of Umm Al Quwain Fort in 1768, which today houses the Umm Al Quwain museum. The fort and its watchtower were built after the Al Ali tribe moved from the island of Sinniyah to the mainland due to exhausted water supplies on the island. This strategic move established the permanent settlement that would grow into the modern emirate.
achievements: Built Umm Al Quwain Fort in 1768
Led the Al Ali tribe's migration from Sinniyah Island to the mainland
Established Umm Al Quwain as an independent sheikhdom

figures.name: Sheikh Abdullah bin Rashid Al Mualla
figures.title: Ruler of Umm Al Quwain
figures.roleDescription: Second ruler of Umm Al Quwain (1820-1853)
figures.significance: Continued his father's legacy and consolidated the Al Mualla rule during the early British protectorate period.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Abdullah bin Rashid Al Mualla succeeded his father Sheikh Rashid bin Majid as the second ruler of Umm Al Quwain, governing from 1820 to 1853. His reign coincided with the beginning of British involvement in the Persian Gulf region and the establishment of the Trucial States system.

During his 33-year rule, Sheikh Abdullah worked to maintain Umm Al Quwain's independence while navigating the changing political landscape of the Gulf. His long reign provided stability for the emirate during a period of significant regional transformation, including the British maritime truces that would define the relationship between the Gulf sheikhdoms and Britain for over a century.
achievements: Ruled for 33 years, providing stability during British protectorate establishment
Maintained Umm Al Quwain's independence during regional political changes

figures.name: Sheikh Ali bin Abdullah Al Mualla
figures.title: Ruler of Umm Al Quwain
figures.roleDescription: Third ruler of Umm Al Quwain (1853-1873)
figures.significance: Ruled during the mid-19th century period of British influence in the Persian Gulf.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Ali bin Abdullah Al Mualla served as the third ruler of Umm Al Quwain from 1853 to 1873, succeeding his father Sheikh Abdullah bin Rashid. His twenty-year reign occurred during a period of increasing British influence in the Persian Gulf region.

During his rule, Sheikh Ali continued the policies of his predecessors in maintaining Umm Al Quwain's position within the Trucial States system. His reign was marked by the continuation of traditional governance structures while adapting to the evolving relationship with British authorities in the region.
achievements: Maintained stability during 20 years of rule
Continued the Al Mualla dynasty's governance traditions

figures.name: Sheikh Ahmad bin Abdullah Al Mualla
figures.title: Ruler of Umm Al Quwain
figures.roleDescription: Fourth ruler of Umm Al Quwain (1873-1904)
figures.significance: Ruled during the transition from the 19th to 20th century, overseeing Umm Al Quwain's development during the late Ottoman period.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Ahmad bin Abdullah Al Mualla was born in 1844 and served as the fourth ruler of Umm Al Quwain from 1873 until his death in 1904. His 31-year reign spanned the late 19th and early 20th centuries, a period of significant change in the Persian Gulf region.

During his rule, Sheikh Ahmad navigated the challenges of modernization while maintaining traditional governance structures. His long reign provided continuity for Umm Al Quwain during a period when the Gulf emirates were experiencing increasing contact with the outside world through trade and diplomatic relations with the British.
achievements: Ruled for 31 years during a period of regional transformation
Maintained Umm Al Quwain's stability during the late Ottoman period

figures.name: Sheikh Rashid bin Ahmad Al Mualla
figures.title: Ruler of Umm Al Quwain
figures.roleDescription: Fifth ruler of Umm Al Quwain (1904-1922)
figures.significance: Ruled during World War I and the early 20th century modernization period.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Rashid bin Ahmad Al Mualla was born in 1876 and became the fifth ruler of Umm Al Quwain in 1904, succeeding his father Sheikh Ahmad bin Abdullah. He ruled for 18 years until his death in 1922, during a period of significant global and regional change.

His reign encompassed World War I and its aftermath, a time when the Persian Gulf region experienced considerable political and economic transformation. Sheikh Rashid worked to maintain Umm Al Quwain's position within the British protectorate system while adapting to the changing circumstances of the early 20th century.
achievements: Maintained stability during World War I period
Adapted traditional governance to early 20th century changes

figures.name: Sheikh Abdullah bin Rashid Al Mualla II
figures.nameAr: الشيخ عبدالله بن راشد المعلا الثاني
figures.title: Ruler of Umm Al Quwain
figures.roleDescription: Sixth ruler of Umm Al Quwain (1922-1923)
figures.significance: Had the shortest reign in Al Mualla history, ruling for only one year.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Abdullah bin Rashid Al Mualla II succeeded his father Sheikh Rashid bin Ahmad in 1922 and ruled until 1923, making his reign the shortest in the history of the Al Mualla dynasty. His brief rule lasted only one year during the post-World War I period.

Despite the brevity of his reign, Sheikh Abdullah maintained the continuity of Al Mualla rule during a transitional period. His short tenure was followed by his successor Sheikh Hamad bin Ibrahim Al Mualla, indicating possible internal succession arrangements within the ruling family.
achievements: Maintained dynastic continuity during transition period

figures.name: Sheikh Hamad bin Ibrahim Al Mualla
figures.title: Ruler of Umm Al Quwain
figures.roleDescription: Seventh ruler of Umm Al Quwain (1923-1929)
figures.significance: Ruled during the interwar period of the 1920s.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Hamad bin Ibrahim Al Mualla served as the seventh ruler of Umm Al Quwain from 1923 to 1929, succeeding Sheikh Abdullah bin Rashid Al Mualla II. His six-year reign occurred during the interwar period of the 1920s.

Sheikh Hamad's rule coincided with a period of economic challenges in the Persian Gulf region, particularly affecting the traditional pearling industry. His reign represented a period of adaptation as the Gulf emirates began to experience the early effects of changing economic conditions that would later be transformed by oil discoveries.
achievements: Guided Umm Al Quwain through the challenging 1920s interwar period
Maintained stability during economic difficulties in the Gulf

figures.name: Sheikh Ahmad bin Rashid Al Mualla
figures.title: Ruler of Umm Al Quwain
figures.roleDescription: Eighth ruler of Umm Al Quwain (1929-1981) and founding father of the UAE
figures.significance: One of the founding fathers of the United Arab Emirates and ruled for 52 years through the formation of the modern state.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Ahmad bin Rashid Al Mualla was born in 1904 and became the eighth ruler of Umm Al Quwain in 1929, ruling until his death in 1981. His remarkable 52-year reign was the longest in Al Mualla dynasty history and encompassed some of the most transformative periods in Gulf history.

Sheikh Ahmad was one of the founding fathers of the United Arab Emirates, participating in the historic union of the seven emirates on December 2, 1971. His long rule saw the transformation of Umm Al Quwain from a traditional pearling and fishing community into a modern emirate within the UAE federation. He worked alongside other founding leaders like Sheikh Zayed of Abu Dhabi and Sheikh Rashid of Dubai to establish the UAE as a unified nation, contributing to the negotiations and agreements that created the modern federal structure.
achievements: One of the seven founding fathers of the UAE in 1971
Longest reign in Al Mualla history at 52 years
Oversaw transformation from traditional to modern emirate
Participated in UAE federation negotiations

figures.name: Sheikh Rashid bin Ahmad Al Mualla II
figures.nameAr: الشيخ راشد بن أحمد المعلا الثاني
figures.title: Ruler of Umm Al Quwain
figures.roleDescription: Ninth ruler of Umm Al Quwain (1981-2009)
figures.significance: Oversaw Umm Al Quwain's development during the UAE's oil boom and modernization period.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Rashid bin Ahmad Al Mualla II was born in 1932 and served as the ninth ruler of Umm Al Quwain from 1981 to 2009, succeeding his father Sheikh Ahmad bin Rashid. His 28-year reign spanned the UAE's most significant period of modernization and economic development.

During his rule, Sheikh Rashid oversaw Umm Al Quwain's participation in the UAE's rapid development following the oil boom. He worked to modernize the emirate's infrastructure while maintaining its traditional character. His reign saw significant improvements in education, healthcare, and economic diversification, contributing to the overall success of the UAE federation during its formative decades.
achievements: Modernized Umm Al Quwain's infrastructure during the oil boom
Maintained emirate's traditional character while embracing development
Contributed to UAE's federal development for 28 years

figures.name: Sheikh Saud bin Rashid Al Mualla
figures.title: Ruler of Umm Al Quwain
figures.roleDescription: Current ruler of Umm Al Quwain (2009-present) and member of UAE Supreme Council
figures.significance: Current ruler leading Umm Al Quwain's continued development and modernization within the UAE.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Saud bin Rashid Al Mualla was born on October 1, 1952, and became the tenth ruler of Umm Al Quwain on January 2, 2009, following the death of his father Sheikh Rashid bin Ahmad Al Mualla II. He is also a member of the Federal Supreme Council of the United Arab Emirates.

Sheikh Saud received his elementary and primary education in Umm Al Quwain before attending high school in Lebanon. He graduated with a degree in economics from Cairo University, Egypt, in 1974. His political career began in 1973 when he was appointed to the UAE foreign ministry and seconded to the Umm Al Quwain Amiri court. He served as Commander of the Umm Al Quwain National Guard and was appointed Crown Prince in 1982. In 2006, he launched the first joint offshore gas project with Ras Al Khaimah. He is married to Sheikha Sumaya bint Saqr Al Qasimi, sister of the ruler of Ras Al Khaimah.
achievements: Launched first joint offshore gas project with Ras Al Khaimah in 2006
Served as Crown Prince for 27 years before becoming ruler
Established numerous government entities and local enterprises
Member of UAE Federal Supreme Council

figures.name: Sheikh Abdullah bin Rashid Al Mualla III
figures.nameAr: الشيخ عبدالله بن راشد المعلا الثالث
figures.title: Deputy Ruler of Umm Al Quwain
figures.roleDescription: Current Deputy Ruler of Umm Al Quwain (2004-present)
figures.significance: Current Deputy Ruler supporting the governance and development of Umm Al Quwain.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Abdullah bin Rashid Al Mualla III was born in 1971 and has served as Deputy Ruler of Umm Al Quwain since April 20, 2004. He holds this important position in the emirate's government structure, supporting the ruler in administrative and governmental affairs.

As Deputy Ruler, Sheikh Abdullah plays a crucial role in the day-to-day governance of Umm Al Quwain and assists in the emirate's continued development within the UAE federation. His position represents the next generation of Al Mualla leadership, ensuring continuity in the emirate's governance structure.
achievements: Served as Deputy Ruler for over 20 years
Supports emirate's governance and development

figures.name: Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi
figures.title: Ruler of Ajman
figures.roleDescription: First Al Nuaimi ruler of Ajman
figures.significance: Founded Al Nuaimi rule over Ajman by taking the coastal settlement from the Al Bu Shamis tribe.
figures.biography: Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi was the founder of Al Nuaimi rule in Ajman. He and fifty of his followers took the coastal settlement of Ajman from members of the Al Bu Shamis tribe in a short conflict. However, it wasn't until 1816 or 1817 that the Ajman fort finally fell to Rashid's followers and his rule was endorsed by the powerful Sheikh of neighbouring Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah, Sheikh Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi. He ruled from 1816 until his death in 1838, establishing the dynasty that continues to rule Ajman to this day.
achievements: Founded Al Nuaimi rule over Ajman
Captured Ajman fort
Received endorsement from Sheikh Sultan bin Saqr Al Qasimi

figures.name: Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi
figures.title: Ruler of Ajman
figures.roleDescription: Second ruler of Ajman, ruled twice
figures.significance: Ruled Ajman during two separate periods, from 1838-1841 and 1848-1864.
figures.biography: Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi was the son of the founder of Al Nuaimi rule and served as the second ruler of Ajman. He had two separate reigns due to the complex succession politics of the time. His first reign lasted from 1838 to 1841, when he was succeeded by his brother Sheikh Abdelaziz bin Rashid Al Nuaimi. After his brother's death in 1848, Humaid returned to power for his second reign, which lasted until his own death in 1864. His rule spanned a total of 19 years across these two periods.
achievements: Ruled Ajman during two separate periods
Maintained Al Nuaimi control over the emirate

figures.name: Sheikh Abdelaziz bin Rashid Al Nuaimi
figures.title: Ruler of Ajman
figures.roleDescription: Third ruler of Ajman
figures.significance: Ruled Ajman from 1841 to 1848 between his brother's two reigns.
figures.biography: Sheikh Abdelaziz bin Rashid Al Nuaimi was the brother of Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi and served as the third ruler of Ajman. He came to power in 1841 when his brother stepped down from his first reign. Abdelaziz ruled for seven years until his death in 1848, at which point power returned to his brother Humaid for the latter's second reign. His rule represented a period of continuity in the early Al Nuaimi dynasty's consolidation of power in Ajman.
achievements: Maintained Al Nuaimi rule during transition period

figures.name: Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi II
figures.title: Ruler of Ajman
figures.roleDescription: Fifth ruler of Ajman
figures.significance: Ruled Ajman for 27 years from 1864 to 1891, one of the longest reigns in the dynasty.
figures.biography: Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi II became the fifth ruler of Ajman following the death of his father Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi in 1864. He ruled for 27 years until April 1891, making his one of the longest reigns in the Al Nuaimi dynasty during the 19th century. His extended rule provided stability to the emirate during a period when the region was experiencing significant changes due to British influence in the Persian Gulf. He was succeeded by Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi II upon his death in 1891.
achievements: Ruled for 27 years
Provided stability during period of regional change

figures.name: Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi II
figures.title: Ruler of Ajman
figures.roleDescription: Sixth ruler of Ajman
figures.significance: Ruled Ajman from April 1891 to July 1900, bridging the 19th and 20th centuries.
figures.biography: Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi II became the sixth ruler of Ajman in April 1891 following the death of Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi II. His reign lasted just over nine years, from April 1891 until his death on 8 July 1900. His rule marked the transition period as the 19th century came to a close and the region entered the 20th century. He was succeeded by Sheikh Abdulaziz bin Humaid Al Nuaimi, continuing the dynasty's rule into the new century.
achievements: Maintained dynasty continuity through turn of century

figures.name: Sheikh Abdulaziz bin Humaid Al Nuaimi
figures.title: Ruler of Ajman
figures.roleDescription: Seventh ruler of Ajman
figures.significance: Ruled Ajman during the first decade of the 20th century from 1900 to 1910.
figures.biography: Sheikh Abdulaziz bin Humaid Al Nuaimi became the seventh ruler of Ajman on 8 July 1900 following the death of Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi II. He ruled for nearly ten years until February 1910, governing the emirate during the first decade of the 20th century. His reign occurred during a period of increasing British influence in the Persian Gulf region and growing modernization pressures. He died in 1910 and was succeeded by Sheikh Humaid bin Abdulaziz Al Nuaimi.
achievements: Ruled during early 20th century modernization period

figures.name: Sheikh Humaid bin Abdulaziz Al Nuaimi
figures.title: Ruler of Ajman
figures.roleDescription: Eighth ruler of Ajman
figures.significance: Ruled Ajman from 1910 to 1928, spanning nearly two decades of the early 20th century.
figures.biography: Sheikh Humaid bin Abdulaziz Al Nuaimi became the eighth ruler of Ajman in February 1910 following the death of his father Sheikh Abdulaziz bin Humaid Al Nuaimi. His reign lasted 18 years until January 1928, making him one of the longer-serving rulers of the dynasty. He governed during a significant period that included World War I and its aftermath, as well as the continued development of British relations with the Trucial States. He was succeeded by Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi III in 1928.
achievements: Ruled for 18 years through World War I period

figures.name: Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi III
figures.title: Ruler of Ajman
figures.roleDescription: Ninth ruler of Ajman
figures.significance: Ruled Ajman for 53 years and was the father of the current ruler, overseeing the formation of the UAE.
figures.biography: Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi III was born in 1904 and became the ninth ruler of Ajman in January 1928, succeeding Sheikh Humaid bin Abdulaziz Al Nuaimi. His remarkable reign lasted 53 years until his death on 6 September 1981, making him the longest-serving ruler in the Al Nuaimi dynasty. During his rule, he witnessed and participated in the transformation of the region from British protectorates to the formation of the United Arab Emirates in December 1971. His reign spanned crucial decades of modernization, oil discovery, and the establishment of the UAE federal system. He was succeeded by his son Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III, who continues to rule today.
achievements: Longest-serving ruler with 53-year reign
Oversaw formation of the UAE
Modernized Ajman during oil era

figures.name: Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III
figures.title: Ruler of Ajman
figures.roleDescription: Current and tenth ruler of Ajman
figures.significance: Current ruler of Ajman since 1981 and member of the UAE Federal Supreme Council.
figures.biography: Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III was born in 1931 and is the current ruler of the Emirate of Ajman, serving as the 10th ruler of the dynasty. He succeeded his father Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi III on 6 September 1981 and had previously served as deputy ruler since 1960. He received his early education in Dubai in the 1940s and 1950s and later went to Cairo for further studies. In the early 1970s he became active in the affairs of state as Ajman joined the United Arab Emirates in December 1971. He serves as a member of the Federal Supreme Council of the UAE and has issued various Emiri decrees modernizing the emirate's administration. His philanthropic efforts include sponsoring Sudanese children orphaned by COVID-19 and supporting educational initiatives.
achievements: Longest-serving current UAE ruler since 1981
Member of UAE Federal Supreme Council
Modernized Ajman's government structure
Active in philanthropic work

figures.name: Sheikh Ammar bin Humaid Al Nuaimi
figures.title: Crown Prince of Ajman
figures.roleDescription: Crown Prince and Chairman of Ajman Executive Council
figures.significance: Crown Prince of Ajman and chairman of the Ajman Executive Council since 2003.
figures.biography: Sheikh Ammar bin Humaid Al Nuaimi was born in 1969 as the son of the current ruler Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III. His mother, Amna Ahmad Ghurair, was the second wife of the ruler and died in 1981. He is a graduate of the Ajman Police Academy and has been serving as Crown Prince of Ajman. Since 2003, he has served as chairman of the Ajman Executive Council, playing a key role in the emirate's governance and development. He is also the owner of Ajman Stud, which he established in 2002, and serves as chairman of Ajman Bank. Additionally, he chairs the Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi Charitable and Humanitarian Foundation. He is married to Asma bint Saqr Al Qasimi, who is the sister of Saud bin Saqr Al Qasimi, the ruler of Ras Al Khaimah.
achievements: Chairman of Ajman Executive Council since 2003
Established Ajman Stud in 2002
Chairman of Ajman Bank
Chairs charitable foundation

figures.name: Sheikh Ahmed bin Humaid Al Nuaimi
figures.title: Chairman of Economic Department
figures.roleDescription: Chairman of Ajman's Economic Department
figures.significance: Son of the current ruler serving as Chairman of the Economic Department in Ajman.
figures.biography: Sheikh Ahmed bin Humaid Al Nuaimi is one of the sons of the current ruler Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III. He serves as the Chairman of the Economic Department in the Emirate of Ajman, playing a key role in the emirate's economic development and policy-making. As a member of the ruling family, he contributes to the governance and modernization efforts of Ajman under his father's rule.
achievements: Chairman of Economic Department

figures.name: Sheikh Abdulaziz bin Humaid Al Nuaimi
figures.title: Chairman of Department of Culture and Information
figures.roleDescription: Chairman of Ajman's Department of Culture and Information
figures.significance: Son of the current ruler overseeing cultural and information affairs in Ajman.
figures.biography: Sheikh Abdulaziz bin Humaid Al Nuaimi is one of the sons of the current ruler Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III. He serves as the Chairman of the Department of Culture and Information in the Emirate of Ajman, responsible for overseeing cultural policies, information dissemination, and cultural development initiatives within the emirate. His role involves promoting Ajman's cultural heritage while supporting modern information and communication strategies.
achievements: Chairman of Department of Culture and Information

figures.name: Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi IV
figures.title: Chairman of Ajman Municipality and Planning
figures.roleDescription: Chairman of multiple departments and President of UAE Football Association
figures.significance: Son of the current ruler with extensive roles including President of the UAE Football Association.
figures.biography: Sheikh Rashid bin Humaid Al Nuaimi IV is one of the sons of the current ruler Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III and Sheikha Fatima bint Zayed. He holds multiple important positions within Ajman and nationally. He serves as Chairman of Ajman Municipality and Planning, overseeing urban development and infrastructure projects in the emirate. Additionally, he is the Chief of Ajman Club and heads R Holding company. He is also the owner of City University College of Ajman, contributing to the emirate's educational sector. At the national level, he serves as President of the UAE Football Association (UAEFA), playing a significant role in the development of football across the United Arab Emirates.
achievements: Chairman of Ajman Municipality and Planning
President of UAE Football Association
Owner of City University College of Ajman
Head of R Holding

figures.name: Sheikha Fatima bint Rashid Al Nuaimi
figures.title: Sheikha
figures.roleDescription: Sister of current ruler and mother of Fujairah's ruler
figures.significance: Sister of the current Ajman ruler and mother of Sheikh Hamad Al Sharqi, the ruler of Fujairah.
figures.biography: Sheikha Fatima bint Rashid Al Nuaimi was the sister of the current ruler of Ajman, Sheikh Humaid bin Rashid Al Nuaimi III. She died on 14 December 2014. Her significance extends beyond Ajman as she was the mother of Sheikh Hamad Al Sharqi, who is the ruler of Fujairah, another emirate in the UAE. This family connection represents important inter-emirate relationships within the UAE's ruling families, as her son rules one emirate while her brother rules another. Her only son's position as ruler of Fujairah demonstrates the interconnected nature of the UAE's royal families.
achievements: Mother of Fujairah's ruler
Connected Al Nuaimi family to Al Sharqi ruling family

figures.name: Rashid Abdullah Al Nuaimi
figures.title: Former Minister of Foreign Affairs
figures.roleDescription: Former UAE Foreign Minister and diplomat
figures.significance: Former Foreign Minister of the UAE and member of the Al Nuaimi ruling family of Ajman.
figures.biography: Rashid Abdullah Al Nuaimi is a member of the ruling family of Ajman and served as the former foreign minister of the United Arab Emirates. He holds a bachelor's degree in petroleum engineering, which he received from the University of Cairo in 1967. He started his career at the department of oil and industrial affairs in the Emirate of Abu Dhabi before joining the Emirati ministry of foreign affairs, where he worked in various capacities until 1975. In 1975, he was named as the director of political affairs department at the foreign ministry, and in 1976, he became undersecretary for foreign affairs. He served as the minister of state for foreign affairs from 1977 to 1990, when Hamdan bin Zayed succeeded him. In recognition of his diplomatic service, he received the Order of the Two Niles from Sudan in 2018 and the Grand Cordon of the Order of the Rising Sun from Japan in 2024.
achievements: UAE Minister of State for Foreign Affairs (1977-1990)
Received Order of the Two Niles from Sudan (2018)
Received Grand Cordon of the Order of the Rising Sun from Japan (2024)
Served as undersecretary for foreign affairs

figures.name: Moudi bint Sabhan Al Sabhan
figures.title: Mother of Rashidi Emir
figures.roleDescription: Mother of Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid
figures.significance: Mother of the tenth Emir of Jabal Shammar and previously married to Muhammad bin Abdullah Al Rashid.
figures.biography:
Moudi bint Sabhan Al Sabhan was a prominent member of the Al Sabhan family who became connected to the Rashidi dynasty through marriage. She was previously married to Muhammad bin Abdullah Al Rashid before marrying Abdulaziz bin Mutaib Al Rashid, the reigning Rashidi emir. Through her marriage to Abdulaziz, she became the mother of Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid, who would later become the tenth Emir of Jabal Shammar between 1908 and 1920.

Her son Saud was born around 1898, and when his father was killed in the Battle of Rawdat Muhanna in 1906, the Al Sabhan family played a crucial role in protecting and supporting the young prince. Her family's influence in Rashidi politics became evident when her brother Hamoud bin Sabhan Al Sabhan took Saud to safety in Medina and later helped install him as emir in 1908.
achievements: Mother of Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid
Connected Al Sabhan family to Rashidi dynasty

figures.name: Hamoud bin Sabhan Al Sabhan
figures.title: Regent of Jabal Shammar
figures.roleDescription: Regent ruler for his nephew Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid
figures.significance: Served as regent for the Emirate of Jabal Shammar and played a crucial role in installing his young nephew as emir.
figures.biography:
Hamoud bin Sabhan Al Sabhan was a key political figure from the Al Sabhan family who played a decisive role in Rashidi politics during the early 20th century. When his nephew Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid was threatened following his father's death in 1906 and the subsequent killing of his half-brothers, Hamoud took the young prince to safety in Medina. This action proved crucial in preserving the line of succession.

In September 1908, Hamoud returned from Medina and killed Saud bin Hamoud Al Rashid, proclaiming ten-year-old Saud bin Abdulaziz as the new emir of Jabal Shammar. Hamoud then ruled as regent on Saud's behalf until his death in 1909, providing stability during a turbulent period in the emirate's history. His regency marked the beginning of significant Al Sabhan influence in the governance of the Rashidi state.
achievements: Saved his nephew Saud from assassination
Served as regent of Jabal Shammar
Installed Saud bin Abdulaziz as emir

figures.name: Zamil bin Salem Al Sabhan
figures.title: Regent of Jabal Shammar
figures.roleDescription: Second regent ruler for Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid
figures.significance: Served as regent of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar from 1909-1914 and favored maintaining relations with Ibn Saud.
figures.biography:
Zamil bin Salem Al Sabhan succeeded Hamoud bin Sabhan Al Sabhan as regent for the young Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid in 1909. He ruled the Emirate of Jabal Shammar on behalf of the minor emir from 1909 until 1914, during a critical period when the emirate faced increasing pressure from the expanding Saudi state under Ibn Saud.

Zamil's political stance favored maintaining diplomatic relations with Ibn Saud, which put him at odds with his ward Saud bin Abdulaziz, who was pro-Ottoman and opposed to any accommodation with the Saudi leader. This fundamental disagreement over foreign policy led to a conspiracy against Zamil. In 1914, Saud and his cousin Saud bin Saleh Al Sabhan plotted and carried out Zamil's assassination, ending his regency and allowing the young emir to assume direct control of the emirate.
achievements: Served as regent of Jabal Shammar for five years
Maintained stability during turbulent period

figures.name: Saud bin Saleh Al Sabhan
figures.title: Political conspirator and later minister
figures.roleDescription: Conspirator in assassination of regent and later minister to Emir Saud
figures.significance: Participated in the assassination of regent Zamil bin Salem and later became minister under Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz.
figures.biography:
Saud bin Saleh Al Sabhan was a member of the influential Al Sabhan family who played a significant role in the later period of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar. In 1914, he conspired with his cousin Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid to assassinate the regent Zamil bin Salem Al Sabhan. This assassination was motivated by disagreements over foreign policy, particularly Zamil's preference for maintaining relations with Ibn Saud, which conflicted with the emir's pro-Ottoman stance.

After the successful conspiracy and Zamil's death, Saud Al Sabhan became the minister to Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid. In this position, he served the young emir during the final years of the Rashidi state, as it faced increasing pressure from Ibn Saud's expanding Saudi kingdom. His role as minister made him one of the key Al Sabhan family members in the administration of the emirate during its declining years.
achievements: Participated in political conspiracy that changed emirate leadership
Served as minister to the last effective Rashidi emir

figures.name: Fatima bint Zamel Al Sabhan
figures.title: Treasury manager and political influence
figures.roleDescription: Grandmother of Emir Saud who managed the treasury and influenced policy
figures.significance: Managed the treasury of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar and was known for her hatred of the Saudis and Wahhabism.
figures.biography:
Fatima bint Zamel Al Sabhan was a powerful matriarchal figure in the Al Sabhan family who wielded considerable influence during the reign of her grandson, Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid. She held the important position of managing the treasury of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar, giving her significant control over the state's finances during the World War I period.

Fatima was known for her strong political views, particularly her hatred of the Saudis and Wahhabism, which aligned with the emirate's pro-Ottoman stance during World War I. Her influence over her grandson and her control of the treasury made her one of the most powerful women in the emirate during its final years. Her anti-Saudi sentiment likely reinforced the emirate's decision to ally with the Ottoman Empire against Ibn Saud during the war.
achievements: Managed the treasury of Jabal Shammar
Exercised significant political influence over emirate policy

figures.name: Norah bint Hammud Al Sabhan
figures.title: Queen consort of Saudi Arabia
figures.roleDescription: Widow of Rashidi emir who became wife of King Abdulaziz Al Saud
figures.significance: Former widow of Emir Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid who became the eighth wife of King Abdulaziz Al Saud.
figures.biography:
Norah bint Hammud Al Sabhan was originally married to Saud bin Abdulaziz Al Rashid, the tenth and last effective emir of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar. After her husband was assassinated by his cousin Abdullah bin Talal in 1920, she became one of his widows during the final collapse of the Rashidi state.

Following the Saudi conquest of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar in 1921, Norah remarried into the victorious Saudi royal family, becoming the eighth wife of King Abdulaziz Al Saud (Ibn Saud). This marriage represented the integration of prominent Rashidi and Al Sabhan family members into the new Saudi state structure, and was likely part of Ibn Saud's policy of consolidating his rule by incorporating former rival elites through marriage alliances.
achievements: Became queen consort to the founder of modern Saudi Arabia
Represented Al Sabhan family integration into Saudi state

figures.name: Zamil bin Sabhan
figures.title: Early supporter of Rashidi dynasty
figures.roleDescription: Close friend and supporter of the founding Rashidi princes
figures.significance: Early ally who supported the Rashidi brothers in their rise to power in Ha'il.
figures.biography:
Zamil bin Sabhan was an early and influential supporter of the Rashidi dynasty during its founding period in the 1830s. He was described as a cousin and close friend of the founding Rashidi princes Abdullah bin Ali Al Rashid and his brother Prince Ubayd Al Rashid, who led a revolt against the ruler of Ha'il, Muhammad bin Ali of the Jaafar al-Shammari lineage.

Zamil provided crucial support to both brothers during their successful takeover of Ha'il and the establishment of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar in 1836. His backing was particularly important when Abdullah demanded from his brother an ahd (covenant) according to which succession to the office of emir would remain in Abdullah's line. This early support established the Al Sabhan family as key allies and eventually regents of the Rashidi state.
achievements: Supported the founding of the Rashidi dynasty
Helped establish the succession covenant

figures.name: Ibrahim bin Salem Al Sabhan
figures.title: Temporary Emir of Ha'il
figures.roleDescription: Appointed by Ibn Saud as temporary ruler after Saudi conquest
figures.significance: First post-conquest ruler of Ha'il appointed by Ibn Saud to ensure loyalty from local tribes.
figures.biography:
Ibrahim bin Salem Al Sabhan was appointed as the temporary emir of Ha'il by King Abdulaziz Al Saud (Ibn Saud) following the Saudi conquest of the Emirate of Jabal Shammar in 1921. After Ibn Saud gave orders to destroy the historic Barzan Palace and ordered the leaders of both the Rashidi dynasty and Al Sabhan dynasty to relocate from Ha'il to Riyadh, he strategically appointed Ibrahim from the Al Sabhan family to govern the city.

This appointment was a calculated political move by Ibn Saud to ensure the loyalty of the Ha'il people and the Shammar tribe by placing a respected local figure from an established family in charge. Ibrahim's selection represented Ibn Saud's policy of incorporating former regional elites into his administration to maintain stability and legitimacy in newly conquered territories. His role as temporary emir helped facilitate the transition from Rashidi to Saudi rule in the northern Arabian region.
achievements: First Saudi-appointed ruler of Ha'il
Facilitated transition from Rashidi to Saudi rule

figures.name: Mohammed bin Thani
figures.title: Sheikh, ruler of Qatar
figures.roleDescription: First ruler of Qatar from the Al Thani family
figures.significance: Led the Al Thani family's settlement in Doha and became the first ruler of Qatar (1851-1878).
figures.biography:
Mohammed bin Thani was the patriarch who led the Al Thani family's settlement in Doha in the 19th century. The Al Thani family group was named after his father Thani bin Mohammed. Under his leadership, the family moved from their earlier settlements in southern Qatar towns like Sikak, then to Zubarah, Al Ruwais, and Fuwayrit, before finally establishing themselves in Doha.

He served as the ruler of Qatar from 1851 to 1878, marking the beginning of Al Thani rule over Qatar. His leadership established the foundation for what would become the ruling dynasty of Qatar, with the House of Thani continuing to rule the country to the present day.
achievements: First Al Thani ruler of Qatar (1851-1878)
Led family settlement in Doha
Established Al Thani dynasty

figures.name: Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani
figures.title: Sheikh, ruler of Qatar
figures.roleDescription: Second ruler of Qatar, served two separate terms
figures.significance: Consolidated Al Thani rule over Qatar and served as ruler during two separate periods (1878-1898, 1905-1913).
figures.biography:
Sheikh Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani was the son of Mohammed bin Thani and became the second ruler of Qatar from the Al Thani family. He ruled Qatar during two separate periods: first from 1878 to 1898, and then again from 1905 to 1913 after replacing his brother Ahmed who was killed in 1905.

Jassim's reign was significant in consolidating Al Thani authority over Qatar during a crucial period in the region's history. His leadership helped establish the family's political control and laid the groundwork for Qatar's future development as an independent state. The Jassim bin Mohammed Al Thani branch became one of the main family branches within the House of Thani.
achievements: Consolidated Al Thani rule
Founded major family branch

figures.name: Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani
figures.title: Emir of Qatar
figures.roleDescription: Emir of Qatar from 1972 to 1995
figures.significance: Modernized Qatar's government and economy, dramatically increased oil revenues, and established Qatar's modern institutional framework.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani was born in Doha in 1932, the son of Sheikh Hamad bin Abdullah Al Thani and grandson of Emir Abdullah bin Jassim Al Thani. He began his government career in 1957 as Minister of Education, later serving as Deputy Emir, Prime Minister, and Minister of Finance before being named heir apparent in 1960.

On 22 February 1972, Sheikh Khalifa became Emir of Qatar after seizing power from his cousin Ahmad bin Ali Al Thani in a bloodless coup. His reign was marked by significant governmental reorganization, including limiting financial privileges of ruling family members and establishing diplomatic relations with numerous countries. He dramatically increased state revenue from oil through production-sharing agreements with foreign companies and oversaw the commencement of gas production in Qatar's North Field in 1991.

In June 1995, while staying in Geneva, Switzerland, his son Hamad bin Khalifa seized power in another bloodless coup. Khalifa lived in exile in France until returning to Qatar in 2004. He died on 23 October 2016 at age 84, with Qatar declaring three days of national mourning.
achievements: Emir of Qatar (1972-1995)
Modernized Qatar's government structure
Increased oil revenues significantly
Developed Qatar's gas industry

figures.name: Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani
figures.title: Former Emir of Qatar, Father Emir
figures.roleDescription: Emir of Qatar from 1995 to 2013, now known as Father Emir
figures.significance: Transformed Qatar into a major global power through natural gas wealth, founded Al Jazeera, and established Qatar as a regional diplomatic mediator.
figures.biography:
Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani was born in 1952 and graduated from the British Royal Military Academy at Sandhurst in 1971. He served in various military and government positions before being appointed Heir Apparent in 1977. In 1995, he seized power from his father Khalifa in a bloodless coup while his father was vacationing in Geneva, officially becoming Emir and being crowned on 20 June 2000.

During his 18-year reign, Hamad transformed Qatar from a small desert state into a major world power by exploiting the country's vast natural gas reserves. Under his leadership, Qatar's LNG production reached 77 million tonnes, making it the richest country per capita with average income of $86,440 per year. He established the Qatar Investment Authority in 2005, which invested over $100 billion globally in assets including The Shard, Harrods, Paris Saint-Germain F.C., and major stakes in companies like Volkswagen and Barclays.

Hamad founded Al Jazeera news network in 1996 and played crucial roles in regional diplomacy, including mediating conflicts and hosting U.S. military bases while maintaining relations with Iran. In 2010, Qatar was awarded the right to host the 2022 FIFA World Cup. On 25 June 2013, he abdicated in favor of his son Tamim, becoming known as the Father Emir. He has suffered from health issues including kidney problems requiring multiple transplants and is currently on dialysis.
achievements: Emir of Qatar (1995-2013)
Founded Al Jazeera news network
Established Qatar Investment Authority
Secured 2022 FIFA World Cup hosting rights
Transformed Qatar into global power

figures.name: Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani
figures.title: Emir of Qatar
figures.roleDescription: Current Emir of Qatar since 2013
figures.significance: Current ruler of Qatar who succeeded his father in 2013 and leads the House of Thani today.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani was born on 3 June 1980, the fourth son of former Emir Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani and his second wife Sheikha Moza bint Nasser Al-Missned. He served as heir apparent of Qatar from 2003 to 2013 after being designated crown prince by his father.

On 25 June 2013, Tamim became the eighth and current Emir of Qatar when his father abdicated the throne in his favor. As the current leader of the House of Thani, he continues the family's rule over Qatar as a constitutional monarchy with wide-ranging powers. He is married to his first cousin Jawaher bint Hamad Al Thani, with whom he has four children. Under his leadership, Qatar has continued to play an active role in regional diplomacy and international affairs, while preparing to host the 2022 FIFA World Cup.
achievements: Current Emir of Qatar (2013-present)
Heir apparent (2003-2013)
Leads House of Thani

figures.name: Mohammed bin Abdulrahman bin Jassim Al Thani
figures.nameAr: محمد بن عبد الرحمن بن جاسم بن جبر آل ثاني
figures.title: Prime Minister and Minister of Foreign Affairs of Qatar
figures.roleDescription: Current Prime Minister and chief diplomat of Qatar
figures.significance: Leading diplomat who has served as Qatar's Prime Minister since 2023 and Foreign Minister since 2016, playing key roles in regional mediation efforts.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Mohammed bin Abdulrahman bin Jassim Al Thani was born on 1 November 1980 in Doha and raised as a member of Qatar's royal family. He holds a bachelor's degree in economics and business administration from Qatar University (2003) and began his career as an economic researcher at the Ruling Family Council in 2003, later serving as Director of Economic Affairs from 2005 to 2009.

He joined the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in 2013 as Assistant Foreign Minister for International Cooperation Affairs and was appointed Minister of Foreign Affairs in 2016. On 7 March 2023, he was appointed Prime Minister of Qatar through Amiri Decree No. (2) of 2023, while continuing to serve as Foreign Minister. In this dual role, he advises Emir Tamim on government formation and leads cabinet sessions while managing Qatar's foreign policy.

Sheikh Mohammed has played crucial roles in international mediation, particularly during the 2023 Gaza conflict where he facilitated negotiations between Hamas and Israel, coordinating hostage releases and ceasefire agreements. His humanitarian efforts have been praised by global leaders including US President Joe Biden and Secretary of State Antony Blinken. TIME magazine recognized him as one of the '100 Most Influential People' in 2024 for his diplomatic work. He has also served as Chairman of Qatar Fund for Development (2014-2024) and Qatar Investment Authority (2018-2023).
achievements: Prime Minister of Qatar (2023-present)
Minister of Foreign Affairs (2016-present)
Mediated Gaza ceasefire negotiations
TIME 100 Most Influential People (2024)
Chairman of Qatar Investment Authority (2018-2023)

figures.name: Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani
figures.nameAr: عبد الله بن حمد آل ثاني
figures.title: Deputy Emir of Qatar
figures.roleDescription: Deputy Emir and unofficial heir presumptive to the Emir of Qatar
figures.significance: Current Deputy Emir of Qatar and unofficial heir presumptive, appointed by his half-brother Emir Tamim in 2014.
figures.biography:
Abdullah bin Hamad Al Thani was born on 9 February 1988 in Doha, Qatar, as the son of former Emir Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani and his third wife Sheikha Noora bint Khalid Al Thani. He graduated with a Bachelor of Science in Foreign Service from Georgetown University in 2010, demonstrating the family's commitment to international education and diplomacy.

From 20 December 2011 to 10 November 2014, Abdullah served as chief of the Amiri Diwan of the State of Qatar, gaining experience in government administration. On 11 November 2014, his half-brother Emir Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani appointed him to the post of Deputy Emir, unofficially making him heir to the throne of Qatar, though it is expected that Emir Tamim will eventually appoint one of his own sons as crown prince.

In his current roles, Abdullah serves as Chairman of the Board of Trustees of QatarEnergy since 4 November 2018, Chairman of Board of Regents of Qatar University, and Vice-chairman of the Supreme Council for Economic Affairs and Investment. He married Sheikha Al Maha bint Muhammad Al Attiyah at Al Wajbah Palace on 22 March 2012.
achievements: Deputy Emir of Qatar (2014-present)
Chairman of QatarEnergy Board of Trustees
Chairman of Qatar University Board of Regents
Former chief of Amiri Diwan (2011-2014)

figures.name: Jawaher bint Hamad Al Thani
figures.nameAr: جواهر بنت حمد بن سحيم آل ثاني
figures.title: Sheikha, First Wife and Consort of the Emir
figures.roleDescription: First wife and consort of Emir Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani
figures.significance: Current consort of the Emir of Qatar and prominent member of the royal family, representing Qatar in official state functions.
figures.biography:
Sheikha Jawaher bint Hamad bin Suhaim Al Thani was born in Doha in 1984 to Sheikh Hamad bin Suhaim Al Thani, a government minister and member of the Qatari royal family. She is a granddaughter of former Emir Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani, making her a member of the royal family by birth. Her paternal grandfather, Sheikh Suhaim bin Hamad Al Thani, served as Qatar's foreign minister.

She married her first cousin, Tamim bin Hamad Al Thani, on 8 January 2005 at Al Wajbah Palace, before he became Emir. They have four children together: Sheikha Al Mayassa (born 2006), Sheikh Hamad (born 2008), Sheikha Aisha (born 2010), and Sheikh Jassim (born 2012). When her husband succeeded as Emir in 2013, she became his consort.

As consort, Sheikha Jawaher has accompanied her husband on official state visits, including to Spain (2022), the Netherlands (2024), and attended major international events such as Queen Elizabeth II's funeral (2022) and King Charles III's coronation (2023). She serves as royal patron for Qatar University's female graduates and received a Master of Arts degree from Qatar University in 2021. She was awarded the Spanish Order of Isabella the Catholic in 2022.
achievements: First wife and consort of Emir Tamim
Royal patron of Qatar University graduates
Dame Grand Cross of Order of Isabella the Catholic (2022)
Master of Arts from Qatar University (2021)

figures.name: Amrus ibn Yusuf al-Muwallad al-Laridi
figures.title: General and Governor
figures.roleDescription: Muwallad general of the Emirate of Córdoba and governor of Zaragoza
figures.significance: Founded the influential Banu Amrus family and controlled strategic territories in the upper Ebro valley for nearly a decade.
figures.biography:
Amrus ibn Yusuf was a Muwallad (probably of Visigothic origin) general who served the Emirate of Córdoba. A native of Huesca, he and his kinsman Shabrit were mawālī servants of Aysun ibn Sulayman al-Arabi. In 791/792, Amrus turned against his master Matruh al-Arabi during a rebellion, attacking and killing him with swords alongside Sarhabil ibn Saltan al-Zawagi. This act of betrayal brought him favor with the Córdoba court, where he was rewarded with the position of wali of Talavera.

In 802, Amrus was sent from Toledo as a general to suppress another Zaragoza rebellion, successfully taking both Zaragoza and Huesca while expelling Bahlul ibn Marzuq. He established the settlement that would become Tudela, installing his son Yusuf ibn Amrus there. When Zaragoza rebelled again under Fortun ibn Musa in December 802, Amrus was appointed governor of Zaragoza in 803/804, also installing his kinsman Sabrit in Huesca. He successfully quelled a rebellion in Tudela in 807 and occupied the county of Sobrarbe after the death of Oriol of Aragon, territory that remained under his control until Aznar Galíndez I regained it in 814. According to chronicler Al-Udri, Amrus held Zaragoza for nearly ten years, dying in either 808/809 or 813/814.
achievements: Established the settlement that became Tudela
Controlled Zaragoza for nearly a decade
Founded the influential Banu Amrus dynasty
Successfully suppressed multiple rebellions in the upper Ebro valley

figures.name: Mohammed Jaham Abdulaziz Al Kuwari
figures.title: Ambassador and Diplomat
figures.roleDescription: Senior Qatari diplomat and former Ambassador to the United States
figures.significance: Prominent Qatari diplomat who served as Ambassador to the United States from 2014-2016 and previously as Ambassador to France for a decade.
figures.biography:
Mohammed Jaham Al Kuwari was born on 20 May 1958, one of 13 children in his family. He received his bachelor's degree in political science from University of Portland in 1980 and a master's degree in international relations from the University of Madrid in 1990. Al Kuwari began his diplomatic career at the Ministry of Foreign Affairs in 1981 as a Third Secretary, first posted to Washington DC from 1981 to 1986, then Madrid from 1986 to 1990.

He held various senior positions including Deputy Head of Cabinet (1993-1995), Director of European and American Affairs (1995-1997 and 2001-2002), and head of delegation for Qatar's political and strategic dialogue with France (2001-2002). Prior to his appointment as Ambassador to the United States, Al Kuwari served for a decade as Qatar's Ambassador to France and was accredited to Switzerland and the Holy See. In 2007, he became non-resident Ambassador to Monaco and Portugal.

In December 2013, Al Kuwari was appointed Ambassador of Qatar to Washington, DC, officially presenting his credentials to President Barack Obama on 10 March 2014 and serving until December 2016. Throughout his career, he has been recognized with numerous international honors including multiple decorations from France's Legion of Honour, the Grand Cross of the Order of St. Gregory the Great from Vatican City, and the Grand Cross of the Order of Isabella the Catholic from Spain.
achievements: Served as Qatar's Ambassador to the United States (2014-2016)
Served as Qatar's Ambassador to France for a decade
Received Chevalier, Officer, and Commander badges of the Legion of Honour from France
Awarded Grand Cross of the Order of St. Gregory the Great from Vatican City
Received Grand Cross of the Order of Isabella the Catholic from Spain

figures.name: Ali Khalifa Al-Kuwari
figures.title: Democracy Activist and Professor
figures.roleDescription: Qatari democracy activist, author, professor, and oil industry executive
figures.significance: Leading advocate for political reform in Qatar who authored the banned book 'The People Want Reform in Qatar Too' and has been a primary voice for democratic change in the country.
figures.biography:
Ali Khalifa Al-Kuwari was born in 1941 and obtained a BA in business administration from Damascus University in 1966, followed by a PhD in philosophy from Durham University in 1974. He began his career in Qatar's oil and gas industry, serving as vice chairman of Qatar Liquefied Gas Company and the National Company for Petroleum Products, as well as chairman of the Qatar–United Kingdom Joint Committee for Economic Cooperation. In 1959, he was involved in founding Taliaa Club Qatar, a cultural club that held political discussions, which was raided by tanks in 1961 and its members imprisoned.

From 1975 to 1982, Al-Kuwari worked as an economics professor at Qatar University. He co-founded the project Democracy Studies in the Arab Countries in 1991 with Raghid Al-Solh and has published research on the 1963 Qatari nationalist movement, which he traces as the origin of opposition to government policies in Qatar. He hosts monthly meetings known as 'The Monday Meetings' where Qatari citizens debate reform issues.

In 2012, Al-Kuwari published 'The People Want Reform in Qatar Too' after year-long discussions with 60 other Qatari writers forming the 'Qataris for Reform' group. The book criticizes Qatar's economic policy, lack of transparency, education issues, over-reliance on the United States, and declining use of Arabic language. Though permitted for publication, the book was subsequently banned in Qatar and Al-Kuwari's blog was censored. He has authored numerous works criticizing the lack of democracy in Arab countries and advocates for political reform while addressing concerns about Qatar's demographic imbalance between citizens and foreigners.
achievements: Served as vice chairman of Qatar Liquefied Gas Company
Worked as chairman of the Qatar–United Kingdom Joint Committee for Economic Cooperation
Co-founded Democracy Studies in the Arab Countries project in 1991
Published 'The People Want Reform in Qatar Too' advocating for democratic reforms
Served as economics professor at Qatar University (1975-1982)

figures.name: Ghanim Al Kuwari
figures.title: Minister of Information and Culture
figures.roleDescription: Former Qatari government minister
figures.significance: Served as Qatar's minister of information and culture and was involved in a significant political incident in 1985 related to succession disputes within the ruling family.
figures.biography:
Ghanim Al Kuwari served as Qatar's minister of information and culture during the 1980s. He became involved in a major political crisis in 1985 when Sheikh Suhaim bin Hamad Al Thani, brother of Emir Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani, died suddenly of a heart attack in August 1985. Suhaim had been plotting a coup against his brother after learning that Khalifa had named his own son as heir, and maintained his own weapons cache and supporters in northern Qatar.

Following Suhaim's sudden death, his sons blamed Ghanim Al Kuwari for not responding promptly to their father's calls for medical attention during his medical emergency. This accusation led to a serious escalation when Suhaim's sons attempted to assassinate Al Kuwari in retaliation. As a result of this assassination attempt, they were subsequently imprisoned. The incident highlights the complex political dynamics and family rivalries within Qatar's ruling circles during this period.

figures.name: Jafal Al Kuwari
figures.title: Professional Footballer
figures.roleDescription: Qatari football player
figures.significance: Notable Qatari footballer who graduated from Al Sadd's youth academy and represents the success of the club's youth development program.
figures.biography:
Jafal Al Kuwari is a Qatari footballer who graduated from Al Sadd Sports Club's youth academy, one of the most successful football development programs in Qatar. Al Sadd, known as 'The Leader' and the most successful sports club in Qatar with 64 official football championships, has produced many notable local footballers through its comprehensive youth development program.

As a product of Al Sadd's academy, Al Kuwari represents the club's commitment to developing local talent alongside other notable graduates including Khalid Salman, Hassan Al-Haydos, and Khalfan Ibrahim. The Al Sadd youth development program, which had 284 participants enrolled as of 2011, instills specific footballing philosophies and training ideals among young players, contributing significantly to the development of Qatari football talent.
achievements: Graduated from Al Sadd's prestigious youth academy

figures.name: Wedad Al Kuwari
figures.title: Writer and Screenwriter
figures.roleDescription: Prominent Qatari writer specializing in television drama and children's literature
figures.significance: Called 'The Engineer of Gulf Drama,' she is one of the most influential screenwriters in the Gulf region, known for her work in Qatari television and children's theatre.
figures.biography:
Wedad Al Kuwari was born on 21 August 1967 and began her writing career while still in high school, contributing to local magazines including Al Jawhara, Aayam Al Orouba, and Al Aahed. During her high school years, she joined Qatar TV and worked closely with the network's script department. She graduated from Beirut Arab University with degrees in Psychology and Philosophy. Though she briefly acted in plays like 'The Golden Shoe' (1981) and 'The Chamber' (1985), as well as the Syrian miniseries 'Ezz Al Din Al Qassam' (1981), she chose to focus her career on screenwriting.

Her television career took off with the 1987 Qatari series 'Lost,' and she went on to write numerous acclaimed works including 'Yes And No' (2007), which earned her the Best Screenplay Award at the Cairo Arab Media Festival. Her 2009 soap opera 'Hearts for Rent' proved controversial for highlighting social issues like rental crisis and modernity's effects on Qatari society, but also earned her another Best Screenplay Award. She has written for various Gulf productions including Kuwaiti series 'Al Beit Beit Abouna' (2013) and 'Khademat Al Kawm' (2014).

Beyond television, Al Kuwari has been a regular contributor to Emirati women's magazine Zahrat Al Khaleej since the 1990s and Saudi newspaper Al Yaum. She has authored several books including 'I Declare Love,' a collection of articles on education and self-learning, and the biographical novel 'I Lived to Tell.' Many of her works are oriented towards children, including 'The Big House' and 'The Lost One.' In 2019, she wrote beIN Media Group's first drama series 'Ahli Wanasi,' a 32-episode series exploring themes of family conflict and media regulation.
achievements: Won Best Screenplay Award at Cairo Arab Media Festival for 'Yes And No' (2007)
Won Best Screenplay Award at Cairo Arab Media Festival for 'Hearts for Rent' (2009)
Called 'The Engineer of Gulf Drama' and 'Umm Saif'
Wrote beIN Media Group's first drama series 'Ahli Wanasi' (2019)
Regular contributor to major Gulf publications since the 1990s

figures.name: Sheikh Kamal al-Qassab
figures.title: Director of Schools
figures.roleDescription: Educational administrator in Haifa Islamic Society
figures.significance: Served as director of schools for the Haifa Islamic Society until his dismissal in 1932.
figures.biography: Sheikh Kamal al-Qassab served as the director of schools for the Haifa Islamic Society in Palestine during the early 1930s. His dismissal from this position in late 1932 caused significant controversy and led to political ramifications within the Palestinian Arab community. According to the Filastin newspaper, his sacking prompted Rashid al-Haj Ibrahim, a prominent Palestinian Arab leader and banker, to resign from his position in the Haifa Islamic Society in protest. The dismissal was reportedly part of broader political maneuvering, as al-Husayni was allegedly attempting to undermine the rising al-Istiqlal (Independence) party by controlling key institutions like the Islamic Society.
achievements: Served as director of schools for Haifa Islamic Society

figures.name: Hassan Bin Abdulla Al Thawadi
figures.title: CEO of Qatar 2022 World Cup Bid
figures.roleDescription: Chief Executive Officer responsible for Qatar's successful FIFA World Cup bid
figures.significance: Led Qatar's successful bid to host the 2022 FIFA World Cup.
figures.biography:
Hassan Bin Abdulla Al Thawadi served as the Chief Executive Officer of Qatar's bid to host the 2022 FIFA World Cup. He was instrumental in securing Qatar's successful bid for the tournament, one of the most significant sporting events in the world. Al Thawadi comes from the Al-Thawadi family, which is part of the Al-Thawawda (الذواودة) tribe, a sub-clan of the Bani Khalid tribe that has historical presence across Bahrain, Qatar, and Saudi Arabia.

The Al-Thawawda are among the clans that moved to Bahrain from Zubarah following the 1783 Bani Utbah invasion of Bahrain, and the family has maintained significant presence in the Gulf region. Hassan's role in the World Cup bid represents the family's continued involvement in major regional and international initiatives in the modern era.
achievements: Led Qatar's successful bid for the 2022 FIFA World Cup

figures.name: Isa Bin Sultan Al Thawadi
figures.title: Founder of the Housing Bank
figures.roleDescription: Banking sector pioneer and founder
figures.significance: Founded the Housing Bank, contributing to the development of the banking sector in the Gulf region.
figures.biography:
Isa Bin Sultan Al Thawadi was a prominent businessman and banking pioneer who founded the Housing Bank. His establishment of this financial institution represents the Al-Thawadi family's involvement in the modern economic development of the Gulf region. As a member of the Al-Thawawda tribe, a sub-clan of the Bani Khalid, his family has historical roots across Bahrain, Qatar, and Saudi Arabia.

The Al-Thawawda were among the merchant families that moved to Bahrain from Zubarah following the 1783 Bani Utbah invasion, and many family members were historically involved in the lucrative pearl hunting industry.
