Isa Bin Sultan's move into modern banking represents the family's adaptation to changing economic conditions in the Gulf, transitioning from traditional pearl trading to modern financial services.
achievements: Founded the Housing Bank

figures.name: Ahmad Al-Thawadi
figures.title: Political Activist
figures.roleDescription: Leftist political activist
figures.significance: Known for his leftist political activism in the Gulf region.
figures.biography:
Ahmad Al-Thawadi was a prominent leftist political activist from the Al-Thawadi family. He represents the family's engagement with political movements and social change in the modern Gulf region. Coming from the Al-Thawawda tribe, a sub-clan of the Bani Khalid, his family has deep historical roots in the region, particularly in Bahrain where they settled after moving from Zubarah following the 1783 Bani Utbah invasion.

The Al-Thawawda family traditionally had strong involvement in the pearl hunting industry and were part of the merchant community in Bahrain. Ahmad's political activism represents a different path for family members, showing their engagement with ideological and political movements rather than purely commercial activities. His leftist orientation reflects the broader political currents that influenced the Gulf region during the 20th century.
achievements: Recognized as a prominent leftist political activist

figures.name: Bassam Mohammed Al-Thawadi
figures.title: Film Director and Producer
figures.roleDescription: Pioneer filmmaker and director
figures.significance: Regarded as a regional pioneer in film-making and produced Bahrain's first feature film.
figures.biography:
Bassam Mohammed Al-Thawadi was born on December 13, 1960, in Gudaibiya in the Bahraini capital, Manama. He is a veteran Bahraini filmmaker and film director, known for producing Bahrain's first feature film, 'The Barrier,' in 1990. His father worked in nearby Saudi Arabia in the 1960s, and it was during this time that Bassam developed an interest in photography. In 1979, he studied filming at the Higher Institute of Cinema in Giza, Egypt, graduating in 1982.

Bassam joined Bahrain TV on May 4, 1985, and produced a variety of programs for the channel, including programs broadcast across Arab countries through the Arab States Broadcasting Union (ASBU). He directed several notable films including 'Visitor' in 2004, considered Bahrain's second feature film and the first with Dolby sound system in the Persian Gulf, and 'A Bahraini Tale' in 2006, the third feature film produced in Bahrain. He is a founding member of the GCC Cinema Society and founder and director-in-general of the first Arab Cinema Festival in Bahrain. On April 10, 2012, he was awarded the Lifetime Achievement Award at the 5th Gulf Film Festival in recognition of his lifetime work in Gulf film making.
achievements: Produced Bahrain's first feature film 'The Barrier' (1990)
Founded the first Arab Cinema Festival in Bahrain
Founding member of the GCC Cinema Society
Received Lifetime Achievement Award at 5th Gulf Film Festival (2012)

figures.name: Ali al-Thawadi
figures.title: Minister for Strategic Affairs of Qatar
figures.roleDescription: Qatari minister, diplomat and former chief of staff
figures.significance: Served as deputy chief executive for Qatar's successful bid to host the FIFA World Cup and plays a role in Qatar's mediation efforts.
figures.biography:
Ali al-Thawadi is a Qatari minister, diplomat and former chief of staff to Mohammed bin Hamad Al Thani. He served as deputy chief executive for Qatar's successful bid for the 2022 FIFA World Cup, where he oversaw a campaign seeking to influence the bidding process. He has been part of Qatar's role as mediator between Hamas and Israel during the Gaza war and currently sits on the Gaza Executive Board as part of the Board of Peace organization.

Al-Thawadi was involved in negotiations between Hamas and Israel leading to a ceasefire during the Gaza war, contributing to the release of hostages and playing a role in Qatar's contribution to peace efforts. In January 2026, he was appointed as the Qatari representative to the Gaza Executive Board. He previously served as Chief of staff to Mohammed bin Hamad Al Thani, brother of the Emir of Qatar, and is currently serving as Minister at the Prime Minister's Office for strategy affairs.
achievements: Deputy chief executive for Qatar's successful 2022 FIFA World Cup bid
Appointed as Qatari representative to the Gaza Executive Board (2026)
Involved in Hamas-Israel ceasefire negotiations

figures.name: Hafiz Jamal ud din
figures.title: Religious Leader and Village Founder
figures.roleDescription: Spiritual figure who founded the village of Bani Hafiz
figures.significance: Founded the village of Bani Hafiz after receiving land as a gift from the Raja of Salmia in the late 1700s.
figures.biography: Hafiz Jamal ud din was a religious and spiritual person who lived in the late 1700s. The land of Bani Hafiz was given as a gift to him by the Raja of Salmia during this period. He used to meditate in the jungle in Bani Hafiz and was known for his spiritual practices. Hafiz Jamal had two sons: Hafiz Muhammad Ghous and Hafiz Muhammad Jee. His elder son Hafiz Muhammad Ghous shifted to Ban Khawra, a small village in Lamnian on the other side of the river Jehlum, while Hafiz Muhammad Jee stayed at Bani Hafiz and his family propagated there. Urs (religious festival) of Hafiz Jamal and his sons are carried out around the year in Bani Hafiz, with people from distant areas coming to celebrate these festivities, indicating his lasting spiritual influence in the region.
achievements: Founded the village of Bani Hafiz
Established a spiritual legacy commemorated through annual Urs festivals

figures.name: Hafiz Muhammad Jee
figures.title: Religious Leader
figures.roleDescription: Son of Hafiz Jamal ud din who continued the family's spiritual tradition
figures.significance: Stayed in Bani Hafiz and propagated the family lineage, becoming the patriarch of the Bani Hafiz family.
figures.biography: Hafiz Muhammad Jee was one of the two sons of Hafiz Jamal ud din, the founder of Bani Hafiz village. While his elder brother Hafiz Muhammad Ghous moved to Ban Khawra across the river Jehlum, Hafiz Muhammad Jee chose to remain in Bani Hafiz. The family of Hafiz Muhammad Jee propagated in Bani Hafiz, making him the key patriarch of the family's continuation in the village. He had three sons: Hafiz Abdul Qudus (known as Molvi Bajee), Hafiz Muhammad Yunus, and Hafiz Abdul Latif, all of whom stayed and prospered in Bani Hafiz. His lineage continued the spiritual traditions of the family, with annual Urs festivals being held to commemorate him alongside his father.
achievements: Established the main family lineage in Bani Hafiz
Father of three prominent sons who continued the family tradition

figures.name: Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar
figures.title: Politician and Acting President
figures.roleDescription: 17th Acting President of Azad Kashmir and prominent political leader
figures.significance: Served as Acting President of Azad Kashmir and was a founding member of Pakistan People's Party in the region.
figures.biography: Born on 12 September 1944 in Bani Hafiz, a village of Hattian Bala, Azad Kashmir, Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar belonged to the spiritual family of Bani Hafiz. His father was Hafiz Abdul Latif, making him the great-grandson of Hafiz Jamal ud din. Born an orphan, he was raised by his uncle Hafiz Mian Muhammad Yunus, a local Sufi saint. He matriculated from Chikar Tehsil and later went to Karachi to study law, earning a master's degree in political science and Urdu from Karachi University. Before joining politics, he worked as a primary school teacher but left to join Zulfiqar Ali Bhutto's Pakistan People's Party in the early 1970s, becoming one of the founding members in Azad Kashmir. Zaffar served in various high positions including Acting President of Azad Kashmir from 20-29 July 1991, Speaker of AJ&K Legislative Assembly from 1990-1991, and Senior Minister from 1996-2001. He was known for his loyalty to the Bhutto family and accompanied Benazir Bhutto to rallies and election campaigns. He died of a heart attack on 2 September 2006 in Rawalpindi.
achievements: 17th Acting President of Azad Kashmir
Founding member of Pakistan People's Party in Azad Kashmir
Speaker of AJ&K Legislative Assembly
Senior Minister for five years
President of PPP Azad Jammu and Kashmir
figures.birthPlace: Bani Hafiz, Hattian Bala, Azad Kashmir

figures.name: Hafiz Abdul Latif
figures.title: Religious Leader
figures.roleDescription: Son of Hafiz Muhammad Jee and father of Ishaq Zaffar
figures.significance: One of three sons who stayed and prospered in Bani Hafiz, father of prominent politician Ishaq Zaffar.
figures.biography: Hafiz Abdul Latif was one of the three sons of Hafiz Muhammad Jee who stayed and prospered in Bani Hafiz village. He was the father of Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar, who would later become a prominent politician and Acting President of Azad Kashmir. The family belonged to the spiritual lineage of Bani Hafiz, continuing the religious traditions established by his grandfather Hafiz Jamal ud din. His son Ishaq Zaffar was born an orphan, suggesting that Hafiz Abdul Latif died when his son was very young, leading to the child being raised by his uncle Hafiz Mian Muhammad Yunus.
achievements: Continued the spiritual family tradition in Bani Hafiz
Father of a prominent political leader

figures.name: Hafiz Mian Muhammad Yunus
figures.title: Sufi Saint
figures.roleDescription: Local Sufi saint and spiritual leader
figures.significance: Local Sufi saint whose tomb became a shrine in Bani Hafiz village.
figures.biography: Hafiz Mian Muhammad Yunus was one of the three sons of Hafiz Muhammad Jee who stayed and prospered in Bani Hafiz. He became a prominent local Sufi saint whose spiritual influence extended throughout the region. When his nephew Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar was born an orphan, Hafiz Muhammad Yunus raised him, demonstrating his role as a family patriarch and caretaker. His daughter was Anwar Kasur, who became the principal of degree college for girls Hattian Bala, and the institute was later named after her. His tomb became a shrine in the village, indicating his lasting spiritual significance. His son was Sahibzada Irfan Danish, who served as a member of the constituent assembly from 1990 to 1991.
achievements: Became a revered local Sufi saint
His tomb became a shrine in Bani Hafiz
Raised his orphaned nephew who became a prominent politician

figures.name: Sahibzada Irfan Danish
figures.title: Member of Constituent Assembly
figures.roleDescription: Political representative in Azad Kashmir government
figures.significance: Served as member of constituent assembly from 1990 to 1991, continuing the family's political involvement.
figures.biography: Sahibzada Irfan Danish was the son of Hafiz Mian Muhammad Yunus, the prominent Sufi saint of Bani Hafiz. He followed in the family tradition of public service by entering politics and serving as a member of the constituent assembly from 1990 to 1991. He was the cousin of Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar, who also served as a member of the legislative assembly during the same period (1990-1991), showing that both cousins were active in Azad Kashmir politics simultaneously. His political service represented the continued involvement of the Bani Hafiz family in the governance and political affairs of the region.
achievements: Member of constituent assembly of Azad Kashmir (1990-1991)

figures.name: Anwar Kasur
figures.title: Educational Leader
figures.roleDescription: Principal of degree college and educator
figures.significance: Served as principal of degree college for girls Hattian Bala, with the institute named after her in recognition of her contributions to education.
figures.biography: Anwar Kasur was the daughter of Hafiz Muhammad Yunus, the revered Sufi saint of Bani Hafiz. She dedicated her career to education and served as the principal of degree college for girls in Hattian Bala. Her contributions to education were so significant that the institute was later named after her, demonstrating the high regard in which she was held by the community. She was also noted as a poet of merit, showing her intellectual and literary capabilities beyond her educational administration role. She was the sister of politician Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar, indicating that the family produced notable figures in both education and politics.
achievements: Principal of degree college for girls Hattian Bala
Educational institute named in her honor
Recognized as a poet of merit

figures.name: Hafiz Muhammad Shafi
figures.title: Spiritual Leader
figures.roleDescription: Famous saint of Muzaffarabad region
figures.significance: Famous saint of Muzaffarabad region who provides spiritual guidance to many people who visit him.
figures.biography: Hafiz Muhammad Shafi is the son of Hafiz Muhammad Qudous (also known as Molvi Bajee), making him part of the spiritual lineage of the Bani Hafiz family. He has become a famous saint of the Muzaffarabad region, continuing the family's long tradition of spiritual leadership that dates back to his ancestor Hafiz Jamal ud din. Many people come to him seeking spiritual guidance, indicating his significant influence and reputation as a spiritual leader in the region. He is described as being among the spiritual personalities of the vicinity and is a cousin of the late politician Sahibzada Muhammad Ishaq Zaffar. His prominence as a saint demonstrates the continued spiritual influence of the Bani Hafiz family in the region.
achievements: Became a famous saint of Muzaffarabad region
Provides spiritual guidance to many visitors
Continues the family's spiritual tradition

figures.name: Massimo Scali
figures.title: Italian Olympic ice dancing competitor and choreographer
figures.roleDescription: Olympic ice dancing competitor turned skating coach and choreographer
figures.significance: Former Italian Olympic ice dancer who became a prominent figure skating coach and choreographer, working with elite skaters including Alysa Liu.
figures.biography:
Massimo Scali is a former Italian Olympic ice dancing competitor who transitioned into coaching and choreography after his competitive career. He is now based in Oakland, California, where he works as a skating coach and choreographer. Scali began working with American figure skater Alysa Liu on her skating skills and choreography at the end of 2019, helping to develop her artistic presentation alongside her technical abilities.

Scali's coaching expertise extends beyond choreography to include skating skills development. He worked with Liu during her early competitive years, and after her retirement and subsequent comeback, she returned to work with both Scali and coach Phillip DiGuglielmo. Due to being based in the Bay Area while Liu trains in Southern California, Scali primarily works with her remotely, demonstrating his adaptability in modern coaching methods. His background as an Olympic competitor gives him unique insight into the pressures and requirements of elite-level figure skating.
achievements: Competed in Olympic ice dancing for Italy
Coached and choreographed for elite figure skaters
Worked with multiple-time U.S. national champion Alysa Liu

figures.name: Mufarrij ibn Daghfal ibn al-Jarrah
figures.title: Jarrahid Chieftain and Governor of Palestine
figures.roleDescription: Powerful tribal leader who controlled Palestine and founded Jarrahid dominance in the region
figures.significance: Secured the governorship of Palestine from the Fatimids in 973 and established the Jarrahids as the ruling family of the Tayy tribe and controllers of Palestine.
figures.biography:
Mufarrij ibn Daghfal ibn al-Jarrah emerged as the prominent chieftain of the Jarrahids, a branch of the powerful Tayy tribe, in the late 10th century. He first gained prominence as an ally of the Qarmatians before securing the governorship of Palestine from the Fatimid Caliphate in 973, with Ramla as its center, in reward for military services. His father Daghfal ibn al-Jarrah had been an early ally of the Qarmatians and was based in al-Ramla. In 977, Mufarrij captured the Qarmatian-backed commander Alptakin at the Battle of Ramla and handed him over to the Fatimids, collecting a substantial bounty of 100,000 gold dinars.

Mufarrij lost favor with the Fatimids when the Jarrahids plundered Ramla in 981, leading to their expulsion from Palestine. Afterward, the Jarrahids raided Mecca-bound Hajj pilgrim caravans and vacillated between various powers including the Fatimids, Byzantines, and individual Muslim rulers in Syria. By 1011-12, Mufarrij had regained control and the Jarrahids controlled all of interior Palestine up to Tiberias, even declaring their own caliph al-Hasan ibn Ja'far at Ramla in defiance of the Fatimids. This represented the high point of Jarrahid power, though Caliph al-Hakim eventually bribed them to end the rebellion and later had Mufarrij poisoned to death in 1013.
achievements: Secured governorship of Palestine from the Fatimids in 973
Captured Alptakin and collected 100,000 gold dinar bounty
Controlled all interior Palestine up to Tiberias by 1011-12
Declared independent caliph in defiance of the Fatimids
Contributed to restoration of the Church of the Holy Sepulchre

figures.name: Hassan ibn Mufarrij
figures.title: Jarrahid Chieftain and Ruler of Palestine
figures.roleDescription: Son and successor of Mufarrij who led the Tayy confederation and ruled Palestine
figures.significance: Succeeded his father Mufarrij in 1013 and regained control of Palestine, leading the Tayy into an unprecedented alliance with the Kalb and Kilab tribes.
figures.biography:
Hassan ibn Mufarrij succeeded his father as the leading chieftain of the Jarrahids after Mufarrij's death in 1013. Despite initial setbacks when his brothers Ali and Mahmud surrendered to a Fatimid army, Hassan fled but later gained a pardon from Caliph al-Hakim, who restored his father's iqta'at in Palestine. Hassan assisted al-Hakim in expeditions against Aleppo, demonstrating his military capabilities and political pragmatism.

In 1019, Hassan achieved a significant diplomatic breakthrough by entering his tribe into an unprecedented alliance with the Kalb under Sinan ibn Sulayman and the Kilab under Salih ibn Mirdas. According to the pact's terms, the Jarrahids would rule Palestine, while the Kalb and Kilab would rule Damascus and Aleppo respectively. This alliance dominated Syria until its defeat by the Fatimids in 1029 at the Battle of al-Uqhuwana near Lake Tiberias. After this defeat, Hassan established an alliance with the Byzantines in 1030, and the 20,000-strong Tayy relocated their encampments to al-Ruj near Byzantine-held Antioch. Hassan continued to participate in various military campaigns, including the Byzantine defense of Edessa from the Marwanids and Numayrids in 1035/36, and was mentioned as late as 1041 when the Jarrahids had been permitted to re-enter Palestine.
achievements: Regained control of Palestine after his father's death
Formed unprecedented alliance between Tayy, Kalb, and Kilab tribes in 1019
Established alliance with Byzantine Empire in 1030
Led 20,000-strong Tayy migration to al-Ruj near Antioch
Participated in Byzantine defense of Edessa in 1035/36

figures.name: Daghfal ibn al-Jarrah
figures.title: Early Jarrahid Leader
figures.roleDescription: First documented member of the Jarrahid family and ally of the Qarmatians
figures.significance: The first member of the Banu al-Jarrah to be mentioned in historical records and father of the powerful Mufarrij ibn Daghfal.
figures.biography:
Daghfal ibn al-Jarrah was the first member of the Banu al-Jarrah to appear in historical records and served as an important ally of the Qarmatians in the 10th century. He was based in al-Ramla, the center of Jund Filastin (District of Palestine), where he provided safe haven for an officer of the Qarmatian ruler Abu Tahir al-Jannabi when the latter departed to lead an expedition against Fatimid Egypt in 972 CE. Some sources suggest that Hassan ibn al-Jarrah, who was a commander of auxiliaries in the Qarmatian army during a second invasion of Egypt in 974, may have been the same person as Daghfal.

Daghfal's most significant contribution to his family's legacy was as the father of Mufarrij ibn Daghfal, who would become the most powerful Jarrahid chieftain and establish the family's dominance over Palestine. Through his alliance with the Qarmatians and his strategic position in al-Ramla, Daghfal laid the groundwork for his family's future political prominence in the region.
achievements: First documented member of the Jarrahid family in historical records
Provided safe haven for Qarmatian officers in al-Ramla
Established Jarrahid presence in Jund Filastin
Father of the powerful Mufarrij ibn Daghfal

figures.name: Hazim ibn Ali
figures.title: Jarrahid Chieftain
figures.roleDescription: Nephew of Hassan ibn Mufarrij and later Jarrahid leader
figures.significance: One of the later Jarrahid chieftains who attempted to wrest control of Damascus and became an ancestor of the Al Fadl dynasty.
figures.biography:
Hazim ibn Ali was a nephew of Hassan ibn Mufarrij and emerged as one of the later Jarrahid chieftains in the 11th century. In 1065/66, he and his cousin Humayd ibn Mahmud likely backed Abd al-Sharif ibn Abi'l Jann in his attempt to wrest control of Damascus from the troops of Fatimid vizier Badr al-Jamali. This political venture ended in failure, and both nephews were captured and imprisoned in Cairo. Their release was requested by the Fatimid general and descendant of the Hamdanids, Nasir al-Dawla ibn Hamdan, in 1066/67, suggesting they maintained some political importance even in captivity.

Hazim had sons named Badr and Rabi'a, with the latter becoming significant in the continuation of the Jarrahid lineage. Rabi'a served as an emir of Bedouin auxiliaries for the Burid ruler of Damascus, Toghtekin, who ruled from 1103 to 1128. Through Rabi'a's sons, particularly Fadl ibn Rabi'ah, Hazim became an ancestor of the Al Fadl dynasty, whose emirs would dominate the Bedouin of the steppe until the 18th century.
achievements: Attempted to wrest control of Damascus from Fatimid forces
Ancestor of Rabi'a and the Al Fadl dynasty
Maintained political significance even after imprisonment

figures.name: Humayd ibn Mahmud
figures.title: Jarrahid Chieftain
figures.roleDescription: Nephew of Hassan ibn Mufarrij and political ally of Hazim ibn Ali
figures.significance: Participated in the attempt to control Damascus alongside Hazim ibn Ali and represented the later generation of Jarrahid leadership.
figures.biography:
Humayd ibn Mahmud was another nephew of Hassan ibn Mufarrij who played a role in the later political activities of the Jarrahid family. Like his cousin Hazim ibn Ali, he was involved in the attempt to wrest control of Damascus from the Fatimid forces in 1065/66, supporting Abd al-Sharif ibn Abi'l Jann in this endeavor. The venture proved unsuccessful, resulting in both Humayd and Hazim being captured and imprisoned in Cairo by the troops of Fatimid vizier Badr al-Jamali.

Their imprisonment demonstrated the continued political ambitions of the Jarrahids even after the peak of their power under Mufarrij and Hassan. The fact that their release was specifically requested by Nasir al-Dawla ibn Hamdan, a prominent Fatimid general, in 1066/67 suggests that they retained some political value and connections even in captivity. Humayd represented the later generation of Jarrahid leaders who attempted to maintain their family's political influence in the changing landscape of 11th-century Syria and Palestine.
achievements: Participated in attempt to control Damascus alongside Hazim ibn Ali
Maintained political connections with prominent Fatimid officials

figures.name: Fadl ibn Rabi'ah
figures.title: Jarrahid Emir and Progenitor of Al Fadl Dynasty
figures.roleDescription: Later Jarrahid leader who founded the Al Fadl dynasty
figures.significance: Became the progenitor of the Al Fadl dynasty whose emirs came to dominate the Bedouin of the steppe until the 18th century.
figures.biography:
Fadl ibn Rabi'ah was a grandson of Hazim ibn Ali and represented the later evolution of Jarrahid power in the 11th and early 12th centuries. According to the 13th-century chronicle of Ibn al-Athir, Fadl was described as an emir who, in 1107/08, demonstrated the characteristic Jarrahid pattern of shifting allegiances between various regional powers. He vacillated between the Crusaders, who had conquered the Levantine coast in 1099, and the Fatimids, whose rule had been limited to Egypt since 1071. This diplomatic maneuvering prompted Toghtekin, the Burid ruler of Damascus, to expel Fadl from Syria.

After his expulsion, Fadl formed an alliance with Sadaqa ibn Mansur, the chieftain of the Arab Mazyadid dynasty in Iraq, before ultimately defecting to the Seljuks. His entry into Anbar to block the desert route to Sadaqa marked what Ibn al-Athir described as 'the last that was heard of him' in terms of his individual activities. However, Fadl's true historical significance lay in his role as the founder of the Al Fadl clan, which, together with the Al Mira and Al Faraj clans descended from his brothers, formed the Banu Rabi'a. These clans collectively dominated the desert and steppe regions from the Euphrates valley to central Najd and northern Hejaz, with the Al Fadl eventually becoming hereditary holders of the amir al-'arab position under the Mamluks.
achievements: Founded the Al Fadl dynasty
Maintained political flexibility between Crusaders, Fatimids, and Seljuks
Became progenitor of a dynasty that dominated Bedouin politics until the 18th century
Established alliances with the Mazyadid dynasty in Iraq

figures.name: Ibrahim Nonoo
figures.title: Family Patriarch and Businessman
figures.roleDescription: Founder of the Nonoo family business in Bahrain
figures.significance: Founded the Nonoo family's financial business in Bahrain after migrating from Baghdad in 1888.
figures.biography:
Ibrahim Nonoo was Houda Nonoo's grandfather who left Baghdad in 1888 and started a financial business in Bahrain. He was part of the early wave of Iraqi Jewish migration to Bahrain in the 1880s that established the modern Bahraini Jewish community. His migration was part of the broader movement of Jewish traders seeking economic opportunities in the Persian Gulf region.

The Nonoo family traces its origins to Basra, Iraq, and Ibrahim's establishment of business operations in Bahrain laid the foundation for what would become a prominent merchant family. His descendants would go on to play significant roles in Bahraini politics and society over the subsequent century.
achievements: Established Nonoo family financial business in Bahrain
Pioneer of Iraqi Jewish community in Bahrain

figures.name: Abraham Nonoo
figures.title: Municipal Council Member
figures.roleDescription: First Jewish member of Bahraini municipal government
figures.significance: Served as a member of the Manama Municipality in 1934, becoming the first Jewish person in the Nonoo family to enter Bahraini politics.
figures.biography:
Abraham Nonoo was Houda Nonoo's grandfather who made history in 1934 by serving as a member of the Manama Municipality, the first ever elected municipal body in Bahrain. His appointment was significant as he was not only the first person in the Nonoo family to enter Bahraini politics, but also represented the Jewish community's integration into Bahraini civic life.

His service on the municipal council established a precedent for Jewish participation in Bahraini government that would continue with future generations of the Nonoo family. This early political involvement demonstrated the level of tolerance and inclusion that characterized Bahrain's approach to its minority communities during this period.
achievements: First Jewish member of Manama Municipality
First Nonoo family member in Bahraini politics

figures.name: Ebrahim Daoud Nonoo
figures.title: Businessman and Politician
figures.roleDescription: First Jewish member of Bahraini Parliament's Shura Council and CEO of Basma Company
figures.significance: First Jewish member of the Bahraini Parliament's Shura Council and current head of the Jewish community of Bahrain.
figures.biography:
Ebrahim Daoud Nonoo was born in 1960 and became a pioneering figure in Bahraini politics as the first Jewish member of the appointed upper house of the Bahraini Parliament's Shura Council, serving from 2001 to 2006. He currently serves as CEO of the Basma Company, which offers various office services from IT to janitoring. The Nonoo family originates from Basra, Iraq, having moved to Bahrain over a century ago along with hundreds of Jews from Iraq who sought economic opportunities.

Beyond his business and political roles, Nonoo serves as the head of the House of the Ten Commandments Synagogue and as the head of the Jewish community of Bahrain. He is also president of the Association of Gulf Jewish Communities (AGJC). In 2006, he financed repairs to the country's only synagogue, demonstrating his commitment to preserving Bahrain's Jewish heritage. His appointment to the Shura Council was part of Bahrain's tradition of including minority community representatives in government.
achievements: First Jewish member of Bahraini Shura Council
Head of Bahraini Jewish community
President of Association of Gulf Jewish Communities
Financed synagogue repairs in 2006

figures.name: Houda Ezra Ebrahim Nonoo
figures.title: Ambassador and Politician
figures.roleDescription: First Jewish ambassador from any Middle Eastern Arab country
figures.significance: First Jewish ambassador of any Middle Eastern Arab country, serving as Bahraini Ambassador to the United States from 2008 to 2013.
figures.biography:
Houda Ezra Ebrahim Nonoo was born on September 7, 1964, in Manama to a family of Jewish business entrepreneurs with origins in present-day Iraq. After her father died in a car accident, she returned to Bahrain to take over the Basma Company, becoming a successful businesswoman. She lived for an extended period in the United Kingdom, where she attended Carmel College, a Jewish boarding school, and earned an MBA. She married Salman Idafar, a British Jew, with whom she had two sons, Menasheh and Ezra.

In 2004, she founded and presided over the Bahrain Human Rights Watch Society, advocating for women's rights and foreign workers' rights in Bahrain. She was appointed to the Majlis al-shura in 2005 by King Hamad ibn Isa Al Khalifah, serving for three years as a member of the 40-member Shura Council. On July 3, 2008, she was appointed Ambassador of the Kingdom of Bahrain to the United States, becoming the first Jew and third woman to serve as Bahraini ambassador, and notably the first Jewish ambassador from any Middle Eastern Arab country. Her appointment was responsible for diplomatic representation to Canada, Mexico, Brazil, and Argentina as well. During her tenure, she introduced interfaith initiatives and changed embassy traditions to be more inclusive. Her role ended in November 2013 when she was replaced by Abdullah bin Mohammad bin Rashed Al Khalifa.
achievements: First Jewish ambassador from any Middle Eastern Arab country
Founded Bahrain Human Rights Watch Society
First Jewish member of Bahraini Shura Council
Introduced interfaith diplomatic initiatives

figures.name: Nancy Dinah Elly Khedouri
figures.title: Politician and Author
figures.roleDescription: Member of Bahraini National Assembly and historian of Bahraini Jewish community
figures.significance: Member of the National Assembly of Bahrain since 2010 and author documenting the history of Bahraini Jews.
figures.biography:
Nancy Dinah Elly Khedouri was born in 1975 into the Khedouri family, one of the Jewish families in Bahrain involved in importing tablecloths and linens. She is third generation in Bahrain and is the cousin of Houda Nonoo, the former Bahraini Ambassador to the United States. Her family represents one of the established Jewish merchant families that have been part of Bahrain's commercial and social fabric.

In 2007, she published 'From Our Beginning to Present Day' (Arabic: بداياتنا و حتى وقتنا الحاضر), documenting the history of Bahraini Jews from the first settlers in the late 1880s to present day. In 2010, she became a member of the National Assembly of Bahrain, replacing Houda Nonoo in parliament. She has worked on improving relationships with Israel, appearing publicly with political figures such as Yisrael Katz, and represented Bahrain at the World Jewish Congress in April 2017. Her book emphasized Bahrain's religious tolerance and the peaceful coexistence between Jewish and Muslim communities in the kingdom.
achievements: Author of 'From Our Beginning to Present Day'
Member of National Assembly of Bahrain since 2010
Represented Bahrain at World Jewish Congress

figures.name: Misha Nonoo
figures.title: Fashion Designer
figures.roleDescription: International fashion designer and member of royal social circles
figures.significance: Bahraini and British fashion designer known for her eponymous women's ready-to-wear line and connections to British royalty.
figures.biography:
Misha Nonoo was born in Bahrain to a Jewish father of Iraqi descent and an English mother. Her family moved to London when she was 11, where she attended ACS Cobham School and Guildford High School in Surrey. During high school, she worked as a fashion model represented by Nevs Agency before moving to New York City in 2009 to work in menswear for a tailoring company. She launched her own fashion label in 2011 after being discovered at Prune restaurant by a senior buyer from Intermix wearing one of her own designs, leading to a $150,000 purchase order.

Nonoo became a finalist in the 2013 Vogue/CFDA Fashion Fund and was named to Forbes' 30 Under 30 in Art & Style list and Crain's New York Business' 40 under 40 in 2015. She gained significant media attention in 2015 for producing the first fashion show exclusively on Instagram during New York Fashion Week. In 2019, she collaborated with Meghan, Duchess of Sussex on a clothing collection for the women's charity Smart Works. She married Alexander Gilkes in Venice in 2012 with Lana Del Rey performing at the wedding, divorcing in 2017. In 2019, she married Michael Hess, heir to the Hess Oil fortune, in a high-profile ceremony in Rome attended by Prince Harry, Meghan Markle, and numerous celebrities. Her cousin Houda Nonoo was the Kingdom of Bahrain's ambassador to the US.
achievements: Vogue/CFDA Fashion Fund finalist
Forbes 30 Under 30
First Instagram-exclusive fashion show
Collaboration with Duchess of Sussex

figures.name: Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi
figures.title: Mother of the Sudairi Seven
figures.roleDescription: Most influential wife of King Abdulaziz and matriarch of the Sudairi Seven
figures.significance: She was the most valued spouse of King Abdulaziz, bearing seven sons who became the most powerful group of full brothers in Saudi royal history.
figures.biography:
Hussa bint Ahmed was born in Al Ghat in 1900 to Ahmed bin Mohammed Al Sudairi, a powerful chief of the Sudairi tribe and early supporter of King Abdulaziz. She married King Abdulaziz twice - first in 1913 when she was thirteen, then again in 1920 after a brief marriage to King Abdulaziz's half-brother Muhammad bin Abdul Rahman. Her second marriage to King Abdulaziz produced seven sons known as the Sudairi Seven and four daughters.

Hussa was described as very beautiful with charm and a strong personality. She was influential in King Abdulaziz's decisions and urged him to make Prince Fahd a member of his advisory board. She organized daily dinner gatherings at her home for her sons and their families to maintain unity among them. Bandar bin Sultan described her as a combination of Margaret Thatcher and Mother Teresa. She died in Riyadh in 1969 at age 69, with funeral prayers attended by King Faisal and Crown Prince Khalid.
achievements: Mother of seven sons who became powerful Saudi princes
Organized family unity gatherings that strengthened the Sudairi faction
Most influential wife of King Abdulaziz
Raised Bandar bin Sultan, future Saudi ambassador to the US

figures.name: Fahd bin Abdulaziz Al Saud
figures.title: King of Saudi Arabia
figures.roleDescription: Fifth King of Saudi Arabia and leader of the Sudairi Seven
figures.significance: The oldest of the Sudairi Seven who served as King of Saudi Arabia from 1982 to 2005 and led the most powerful faction within the royal family.
figures.biography:
Born in 1921, Fahd was the eldest of the Sudairi Seven and became one of the most influential Saudi princes. He served as Interior Minister from 1962 and was appointed Crown Prince in 1975 following King Faisal's assassination. When King Khalid died in 1982, Fahd ascended to the throne as the fifth King of Saudi Arabia.

As king, Fahd replaced the royal title of 'His Majesty' with 'Custodian of the Two Holy Mosques' in 1986. A stroke in 1995 left him largely incapacitated, and his half-brother Crown Prince Abdullah gradually took over most responsibilities until Fahd's death in August 2005. During his reign, the Sudairi Seven's influence grew constantly, representing one out of five of King Abdulaziz's sons but wielding disproportionate power due to their political focus and coordination.
achievements: King of Saudi Arabia (1982-2005)
Crown Prince (1975-1982)
Interior Minister (1962-1975)
Introduced the title 'Custodian of the Two Holy Mosques'

figures.name: Salman bin Abdulaziz Al Saud
figures.title: King of Saudi Arabia
figures.roleDescription: Current King of Saudi Arabia and last surviving politically active member of the Sudairi Seven
figures.significance: The sixth-oldest of the Sudairi Seven who succeeded King Abdullah as king in 2015 and consolidated power on behalf of the Sudairi clan.
figures.biography:
Born on December 31, 1935, Salman was appointed Governor of Riyadh in 1962 by Crown Prince Faisal, a position he held for nearly five decades. He became one of the key members of the Sudairi Seven and was appointed Minister of Defense in 2011 following Prince Sultan's death. When Prince Nayef died in 2012, Salman was named Crown Prince.

Salman became King on January 23, 2015, following King Abdullah's death. He immediately began consolidating power on behalf of the Sudairi clan, appointing his son Mohammad bin Salman as Minister of Defense and later Crown Prince. King Salman ended the traditional brother-to-brother succession by elevating his nephew Muhammad bin Nayef to Crown Prince in 2015, then replacing him with his own son Mohammad bin Salman in 2017. As of 2024, Salman and his youngest brother Ahmed are the only surviving members of the original Sudairi Seven.
achievements: King of Saudi Arabia (2015-present)
Governor of Riyadh Province (1962-2011)
Minister of Defense (2011-2015)
Consolidated Sudairi power in the succession

figures.name: Sultan bin Abdulaziz Al Saud
figures.title: Crown Prince of Saudi Arabia
figures.roleDescription: Defense Minister for nearly five decades and Crown Prince
figures.significance: The second-oldest of the Sudairi Seven who served as Defense Minister from 1962 to 2011 and Crown Prince from 2005 until his death.
figures.biography:
Born in 1928, Sultan was appointed Defense Minister by Crown Prince Faisal in 1962, a position he held for nearly five decades until his death. He became one of the most powerful princes in Saudi Arabia, controlling the defense portfolio and building extensive networks within the military establishment. Sultan appointed his younger Sudairi brother Abdul Rahman and his own son Khalid as his deputies in the ministry.

When King Fahd died in 2005, Sultan was appointed Crown Prince by King Abdullah and served as heir apparent until his death in October 2011. His son Prince Bandar served for two decades as Saudi ambassador to Washington and later headed the Saudi National Security Council. Another son, Prince Khalid, served as co-commander with U.S. General Norman Schwarzkopf in the Gulf War and became vice defense minister. Sultan became the leader of the Sudairi faction after King Fahd's death in 2005.
achievements: Defense Minister (1962-2011)
Crown Prince (2005-2011)
Built powerful military network
Father of influential princes including Bandar and Khalid

figures.name: Nayef bin Abdulaziz Al Saud
figures.title: Crown Prince of Saudi Arabia
figures.roleDescription: Interior Minister for nearly four decades and Crown Prince
figures.significance: The fourth-oldest of the Sudairi Seven who controlled internal security as Interior Minister and briefly served as Crown Prince before his death.
figures.biography:
Born in 1934, Nayef was appointed Interior Minister in 1975, succeeding his brother Fahd in the position. He held this crucial portfolio for nearly four decades, controlling internal security, police forces, and domestic intelligence. Like his brothers, he appointed family members to key positions, making his son Muhammad his deputy at the Interior Ministry.

When Crown Prince Sultan died in October 2011, Nayef was appointed Crown Prince by King Abdullah on October 28, 2011, making him second in line to the throne. However, his tenure as heir apparent was brief - he died in Geneva on June 16, 2012, before he could ascend to the throne. His son Muhammad bin Nayef would later become Crown Prince under King Salman from 2015 to 2017, continuing the family's influence in security matters.
achievements: Interior Minister (1975-2012)
Crown Prince (2011-2012)
Controlled internal security apparatus
Father of Muhammad bin Nayef, future Crown Prince

figures.name: Abdul Rahman bin Abdulaziz Al Saud
figures.title: Deputy Minister of Defense and Aviation
figures.roleDescription: Long-serving deputy defense minister and member of the Sudairi Seven
figures.significance: The third-oldest of the Sudairi Seven who served as Deputy Minister of Defense under his brother Sultan for over three decades.
figures.biography:
Born in 1931, Abdul Rahman was appointed as Deputy Minister of Defense and Aviation under his full brother Prince Sultan, serving in this position from 1978 to 2011. His appointment was part of the Sudairi consolidation of power within key government portfolios, as the brothers placed family members in strategic positions within their respective ministries.

Abdul Rahman was reportedly disappointed when he was replaced by Prince Sultan's son Prince Khalid as deputy defense minister in 2011, as he had argued that he should have been promoted instead of his younger brother Salman when government reshuffles occurred. He was removed from the line of succession and died in 2017. Following his death, only the two youngest of the Sudairi Seven - Salman and Ahmed - remained alive, with Ahmed under house arrest by order of King Salman.
achievements: Deputy Minister of Defense and Aviation (1978-2011)
Served under brother Sultan for over three decades
Part of Sudairi consolidation of defense portfolio

figures.name: Ahmed bin Abdulaziz Al Saud
figures.title: Former Interior Minister
figures.roleDescription: Youngest of the Sudairi Seven and former deputy interior minister
figures.significance: The youngest of the Sudairi Seven who served as deputy interior minister for decades and is currently under house arrest.
figures.biography:
Born in 1942, Ahmed is the youngest of the Sudairi Seven and served as Deputy Minister of Interior under his brother Prince Nayef from 1975 to 2012. When Nayef became Crown Prince in 2011, Ahmed was reportedly concerned that Nayef was actively promoting the interests of his own son Mohammed rather than the broader Sudairi family interests.

When Nayef died in June 2012, Ahmed was briefly appointed Interior Minister on June 18, 2012, but resigned from the post on November 5, 2012, and was succeeded by Mohammed bin Nayef, Nayef's son. Ahmed became one of only three members of the ruling family to oppose Mohammad bin Salman becoming Crown Prince in 2017. As of the research period, Ahmed is under house arrest by order of his brother King Salman, making Salman the only politically active surviving member of the original Sudairi Seven.
achievements: Deputy Minister of Interior (1975-2012)
Interior Minister (June-November 2012)
Longest-serving deputy interior minister
Last surviving Sudairi Seven member besides King Salman

figures.name: Ahmed bin Mohammed Al Sudairi
figures.title: Tribal Chief and Provincial Governor
figures.roleDescription: Powerful Sudairi tribal chief and father of Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi
figures.significance: He was the patriarch of the Sudairi family whose daughter Hussa became the mother of the Sudairi Seven, making him the maternal grandfather of Saudi Arabia's most powerful princely faction.
figures.biography:
Ahmed bin Mohammed Al Sudairi (1869-1936) was a powerful chief of the Sudairi tribe and one of the early supporters of King Abdulaziz during his campaigns to conquer and unify Saudi Arabia. As a tribal leader, he played a crucial role in the political alliances that helped establish the modern Saudi state. Following the formation of the state, King Abdulaziz appointed him as governor of several provinces including Washm, Sudair, Qassim, and Aflaj.

His most lasting legacy came through his daughter Hussa bint Ahmed, who became King Abdulaziz's most influential wife and bore seven sons known as the Sudairi Seven. His other sons were also appointed by King Abdulaziz as governors of various provinces: Turki bin Ahmed governed Asir Province, Abdulaziz bin Ahmed governed Quraiyat al Milh and Wadi Sirhan, Khalid bin Ahmed governed Tabuk and later Najran Province and served as Minister of Agriculture, Muhammad bin Ahmed governed Northern Province, Abdul Rahman bin Ahmad governed Jauf, and Musa'id bin Ahmed governed Jizan Province.
achievements: Powerful chief of Sudairi tribe
Early supporter of King Abdulaziz
Governor of multiple provinces
Father of Hussa bint Ahmed Al Sudairi

figures.name: Abdulla Al Futtaim
figures.title: Founder and Patriarch
figures.roleDescription: Founder of Al-Futtaim Group and controlling shareholder of automotive and retail business
figures.significance: Founded the Al-Futtaim Group in the 1930s which became one of the UAE's largest conglomerates.
figures.biography: Abdulla Al Futtaim founded the Al-Futtaim Group in the 1930s, establishing what would become one of the UAE's most successful merchant families and business empires. Under his leadership, the group expanded rapidly in the 1940s and 1950s, becoming an integrated commercial, industrial, and services organization based in Dubai. The business grew to encompass multiple sectors including automotive, electronics, insurance, services, real estate, retail, industries, and overseas operations. In 2000, following mediation by Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum, the Al-Futtaim family split its business interests, with Abdulla controlling the automotive and mainly retail business while his cousin Majid took control of the property development operations. Abdulla's vision and entrepreneurial spirit laid the foundation for what would become a multi-billion dollar enterprise employing over 44,000 people across eight divisions. His business acumen established the family as one of the most prominent merchant families in the Gulf region.
achievements: Founded Al-Futtaim Group in the 1930s
Established Al Futtaim Motors as exclusive Toyota distributor in UAE in 1955
Built integrated commercial and industrial organization

figures.name: Omar Abdulla Al Futtaim
figures.title: CEO and Vice Chairman
figures.roleDescription: CEO and Vice Chairman of Al-Futtaim Group
figures.significance: Current leader of Al-Futtaim Group, continuing the family business legacy as CEO and Vice Chairman.
figures.biography: Omar Abdulla Al Futtaim is the son of Abdulla Al Futtaim and current CEO and Vice Chairman of the Al-Futtaim Group. He received his bachelor's degree in economics and business studies from Macalester College, Minnesota in 1985, establishing a strong educational foundation for his business career. Omar took on leadership of the family business after the 2000 split that saw his father retain control of the automotive and retail operations while cousin Majid took the property development business. Following the sudden resignation of CEO Robert Willett in January 2011, Omar became board member, CEO and vice chairman of the group, leading the organization into a new era of growth and expansion. Under his leadership, the group has maintained its position as one of the Middle East's leading conglomerates, ranked third in Forbes Middle East's Top 100 Arab Family Businesses in September 2022. Omar has also taken on significant roles in the UAE's financial and business sectors, serving as Vice Chairman of Orient Insurance since 2001, chairman of Emirates Islamic and Al Futtaim HC Securities since 2010, and as a director of the Commercial Bank of Dubai.
achievements: Became CEO and Vice Chairman of Al-Futtaim Group in 2011
Chairman of Emirates Islamic and Al Futtaim HC Securities since 2010
Board Member of Dubai Chamber of Commerce and Industry

figures.name: Majid Al Futtaim
figures.title: Founder and Chairman
figures.roleDescription: Founder of Majid Al Futtaim Group, cousin of Abdulla Al Futtaim
figures.significance: Founded Majid Al Futtaim Group in 1992, creating one of the Middle East's largest retail and property conglomerates.
figures.biography: Majid Al Futtaim was the cousin of Abdulla Al Futtaim and founder of the Majid Al Futtaim Group, one of the Middle East's most successful retail and property development companies. In 1992, Majid left the retail-oriented family business led by his cousin Abdulla and created a new firm to run his real estate and property management concerns, establishing what would become known as Majid Al Futtaim Holding. The formal break-up of the two family business entities was finalized in 2000 when Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum arbitrated an agreement between the two cousins that divided their assets, liabilities and business divisions. Under Majid's leadership, his company became best known as the owner of the Mall of the Emirates in Dubai and as the franchisee of French hypermarket chain Carrefour in the Middle East, North Africa and Central Asia. The company expanded rapidly across the region, developing shopping malls, retail operations, and hotel establishments throughout the MENA region. By 2023, MAF had AED 34.5 billion in revenues and AED 2.7 billion in net profit, and was ranked sixth in Forbes Middle East's Top 100 Arab Family Businesses in September 2022. Majid Al Futtaim died in 2021, leaving behind a retail and property empire that spans multiple countries.
achievements: Founded Majid Al Futtaim Group in 1992
Developed Mall of the Emirates and multiple shopping centers across MENA
Secured Carrefour franchise rights for Middle East, North Africa and Central Asia
Built company to AED 34.5 billion in revenues by 2023

figures.name: Ahmad Al Tayer
figures.title: Politician
figures.roleDescription: Emirati political figure
figures.significance: Listed as a prominent Emirati politician in government service.
figures.biography:
Ahmad Al Tayer is an Emirati political figure who has served in government positions in the United Arab Emirates. He appears among the notable politicians listed in the compilation of prominent Emiratis who have contributed to the UAE's political landscape.

As part of the political establishment, Al Tayer represents the involvement of notable merchant families in the governance and development of the UAE. His inclusion in the list of prominent Emirati politicians indicates his role in the country's political and administrative affairs.

figures.name: Ali Hassan al-Majid
figures.title: Colonel General
figures.roleDescription: Iraqi military officer and politician, Defense Minister and Interior Minister
figures.significance: Notorious Iraqi military officer known as 'Chemical Ali' for his use of chemical weapons against Kurdish populations during the Al-Anfal campaign.
figures.biography:
Ali Hassan al-Majid al-Tikriti was born around 1941 in al-Awja near Tikrit, a first cousin of Saddam Hussein from the Al-Bu Nasir tribe. He worked as a motorcycle messenger and driver in the Iraqi Army from 1959 until the Ba'ath Party seized power in 1968, after which he gained entry into the Military Academy and was commissioned as an Infantry officer. His rise was aided by his cousin Saddam, initially becoming an aide to Defense Minister Hammadi Shihab in the early 1970s, then head of the government's Security Office.

During the late stages of the Iran-Iraq War, al-Majid served as Secretary General of the Northern Bureau of the Ba'ath Party from March 1987 to April 1989, effectively making him Saddam's proconsul in northern Iraq. In this role, he ordered the indiscriminate use of chemical weapons against Kurdish targets during the genocidal Al-Anfal campaign, earning him the nickname 'Chemical Ali.' The campaign resulted in the destruction of 4,000 villages and the killing of an estimated 180,000 Kurds. He later served as military governor of occupied Kuwait during the Gulf War and held various ministerial posts including Defense Minister and Interior Minister.

Following the 2003 invasion of Iraq, al-Majid was captured by American forces on 17 August 2003 in Basra. He was tried by the Iraqi Special Tribunal and received four death sentences for genocide, crimes against humanity, and war crimes. He was executed by hanging on 25 January 2010, having been convicted for his role in the Kurdish genocide, killing Shia Muslims, and the Halabja chemical attack.
achievements: Secretary General of the Northern Bureau of the Ba'ath Party (1987-1989)
Military Governor of Kuwait during Gulf War (1990)
Defense Minister of Iraq
Interior Minister of Iraq

figures.name: Saddam Kamel Hassan al-Majid
figures.title: Head of Republican Guard
figures.roleDescription: Iraqi military officer, first cousin once removed and son-in-law of Saddam Hussein
figures.significance: Head of Iraq's Republican Guard who defected with his brother in 1995 but was killed upon their return in 1996.
figures.biography:
Saddam Kamel Hassan al-Majid was born on 1 June 1960, the first cousin once removed and son-in-law of Iraqi president Saddam Hussein. He was married to Rana Hussein, Saddam's daughter, and was the brother of Hussein Kamel al-Majid, who was married to another of Saddam's daughters, Raghad Hussein. For a time, he served as head of the Republican Guard until he was removed from the position in 1986 in favor of Saddam Hussein's son, Qusay Hussein.

Due to his close resemblance to the Iraqi leader, Saddam Kamel played the part of Saddam Hussein in the propagandistic film 'The Long Days,' which depicted Saddam Hussein's early life and rise to power. In 1995, he defected from Iraq along with his brother Hussein Kamel and their wives, fleeing to Jordan. His brother provided information to UNSCOM, the CIA, and MI6 about Iraq's weapons of mass destruction programs during their exile.

In 1996, believing Saddam's assurances that they had been pardoned for their defection, the brothers returned to Iraq. However, shortly after their return on 23 February 1996, both Saddam Kamel and his brother Hussein Kamel were killed in a prolonged gun battle. According to Iraqi authorities, they were killed by other members of their clan who exercised personal vengeance upon the brothers, possibly as a way for Saddam to circumvent his promise of pardon by masking the executions as a family quarrel.
achievements: Head of Republican Guard
Actor in propaganda film 'The Long Days'
Part-time actor

figures.name: Hussein Kamel al-Majid
figures.roleDescription: Iraqi official, son-in-law of Saddam Hussein
figures.significance: Brother of Saddam Kamel who defected in 1995 and provided intelligence about Iraq's WMD programs to Western agencies before being killed in 1996.
figures.biography:
Hussein Kamel al-Majid was the brother of Saddam Kamel al-Majid and was married to Raghad Hussein, one of Saddam Hussein's daughters. He held a significant position within the Iraqi government and had access to sensitive information about the country's weapons programs. In 1995, he defected from Iraq along with his brother Saddam Kamel and their wives, seeking refuge in Jordan.

During his time in exile, Hussein Kamel provided valuable information to UNSCOM (United Nations Special Commission), the CIA, and MI6 about Iraq's weapons of mass destruction programs. His testimony and intelligence were considered crucial for understanding the extent and nature of Iraq's biological, chemical, and nuclear weapons development efforts during the 1980s and early 1990s.

In 1996, Hussein Kamel and his brother returned to Iraq after receiving assurances from Saddam Hussein that they would be pardoned for their defection. However, on 23 February 1996, shortly after their return, both brothers were killed in what Iraqi authorities described as a gun battle with clan members who considered them traitors. The killings were officially presented as family vengeance, though many believed it was orchestrated by Saddam as a way to eliminate the defectors while maintaining plausible deniability.

figures.name: Saddam Hussein Al-Majid Al-Tikriti
figures.title: President of Iraq
figures.roleDescription: Iraqi politician and revolutionary, President of Iraq from 1979 to 2003
figures.significance: Dominant Iraqi political leader for 35 years who ruled as President from 1979 until his overthrow in 2003, known for domestic modernization efforts and regional conflicts.
figures.biography:
Saddam Hussein Al-Majid Al-Tikriti was born on 28 April 1937 in al-Awja, a small village near Tikrit, to a Sunni Arab family from the Al-Bejat clan of the Al-Bu Nasir tribe. His father, Hussein Abd al-Majid, was from the Al-Majid branch of the Al-Bejat clan. After a difficult childhood, he was raised by his uncle Khairallah Talfah and joined the revolutionary Ba'ath Party in 1957 at age 20. He participated in a failed assassination attempt against Iraqi leader Abdul Karim Qasim in 1959, after which he fled to Egypt where he completed his education.

Saddam rose to power through the Ba'ath Party, becoming vice president in 1968 under Ahmed Hassan al-Bakr and formally assuming the presidency in 1979. During his rule, he implemented significant domestic reforms including nationalization of oil resources, infrastructure development, healthcare and education expansion, and women's rights advancement. Iraq experienced substantial economic growth in the 1970s, with the Iraqi dinar worth more than 3 US dollars at its peak. He also invested heavily in modernizing the country's infrastructure, bringing electricity to nearly every city and establishing one of the Middle East's most advanced healthcare systems.

However, Saddam's presidency was marked by three major military conflicts: the Iran-Iraq War (1980-1988), the invasion of Kuwait leading to the Gulf War (1991), and ongoing conflicts with Kurdish populations including the genocidal Al-Anfal campaign. His regime was characterized by authoritarian rule, with extensive use of surveillance, torture, and repression against political opponents. Following the 2003 US-led invasion of Iraq, he was captured, tried for crimes against humanity, and executed by hanging on 30 December 2006.
achievements: President of Iraq (1979-2003)
Vice President of Iraq (1968-1979)
Nationalized Iraq's oil industry (1972)
Established universal free education system
Developed modern healthcare infrastructure
Promoted women's rights and education
Received UNESCO award for healthcare system development

figures.name: Mahmood Al Zarooni
figures.title: Horse Racing Trainer
figures.roleDescription: Former trainer for Godolphin Stables
figures.significance: Notable horse racing trainer who was involved in a major doping scandal that led to his dismissal from Godolphin Stables.
figures.biography:
Mahmood Al Zarooni served as a trainer for Sheikh Mohammed bin Rashid Al Maktoum's prestigious Godolphin racing operation. He joined Godolphin as a trainer a few years before the scandal that would end his career. Al Zarooni was responsible for training horses at the famous Godolphin Stables in Newmarket, England.

In April 2013, Al Zarooni became the center of what The Economist described as 'the biggest doping scandal in racing history.' He was charged by the British Horseracing Authority with doping after 15 Godolphin horses under his care tested positive for anabolic steroids. Sheikh Mohammed declared he was 'appalled and angered' by the case and announced that the stable would be locked down with immediate effect. Al Zarooni was quickly dismissed from his position, and all 15 horses were later cleared to race. He received an eight-year disqualification from thoroughbred horse racing, effectively ending his career in the sport.

figures.name: Jaber Khoory
figures.title: Business Owner
figures.roleDescription: Household appliances company owner
figures.significance: Notable merchant family member who owns a household appliances company in Abu Dhabi that was featured as a location in The Amazing Race 23.
figures.biography:
Jaber Khoory is a businessman who operates the Jaber Khoory Household Appliances Company located in the Irani Souk area of Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates. His business establishment gained international attention when it served as a clue location during the seventh leg of The Amazing Race 23 in 2013, where teams competing in the reality show had to find their next clue at his company.

The inclusion of his business in this internationally televised program highlights the Al Khoory family's established presence in the UAE's commercial sector. His company's location in the Irani Souk suggests connections to the broader Iranian merchant community that has historically played a significant role in the UAE's trade and commerce development.
achievements: Featured location in The Amazing Race 23

figures.name: Ahmad bin Muhammad bin Hilal Al Dhahiri
figures.nameAr: أحمد بن محمد بن هلال الظاهري
figures.title: Wali of Al Ain
figures.roleDescription: Representative and headman of the Dhawahir tribe appointed by Zayed the Great
figures.significance: He served as Zayed the Great's loyal and effective representative in Al Ain during the consolidation of power in the Buraimi Oasis.
figures.biography:
Ahmad bin Muhammad bin Hilal Al Dhahiri lived in Jimi and served as a crucial figure during the consolidation of Abu Dhabi's control over the Al Ain region in the late 19th century. After Sheikh Zayed bin Khalifa Al Nahyan (known as 'Zayed the Great') successfully conquered the Dhawahir's main settlement 'Ain Dhawahir in 1891, he built a fort there to establish his dominion over the oasis. As part of his strategy to maintain control while respecting local tribal structures, Zayed appointed Ahmad as his wali (governor) and made him the headman of the Dhawahir tribe.

Ahmad proved to be both loyal and effective in his role as Zayed's representative. His appointment was significant as it represented a reconciliation between the Al Nahyan ruling family and the Dhawahir tribe, who had previously been in conflict over land and water rights in the oasis. By appointing a respected member of the Dhawahir as his local representative, Zayed was able to maintain stability in the region while ensuring the loyalty of the tribe. Ahmad's position as wali made him responsible for local administration and served as the link between the tribal community and the ruling authority.
achievements: Appointed as Wali of Al Ain by Zayed the Great
Served as effective representative maintaining stability in the region

figures.name: Mohammed Salem Al Dhaheri
figures.title: Chairman of Al Ain Sports Club
figures.roleDescription: First chairman of Al Ain Sports Club after the 1974 merger
figures.significance: He served as the first chairman of Al Ain Sports Club following the merger with Al Tadhamun Club in 1974.
figures.biography:
Mohammed Salem Al Dhaheri became a significant figure in UAE sports history when he was appointed as the first chairman of the board of directors of Al Ain Sports Club in 1974. This appointment came after the historic merger between Al Ain Club and Al Tadhamun Club on 10 November 1974, which consolidated the football scene in Al Ain city. The merger was an important moment in UAE football, bringing together two clubs that had been formed when some members broke away from the original Al Ain club in 1971.

Under Al Dhaheri's leadership, the newly merged Al Ain Sports Club began its journey toward becoming one of the most successful football clubs in the United Arab Emirates. His role as chairman positioned him at the forefront of the club's early development during a crucial period in UAE football history. The club went on to win its first Abu Dhabi League title in 1974, the same year as the merger, establishing a foundation for future success that would see Al Ain become the most trophy-laden club in UAE football.
achievements: First Chairman of Al Ain Sports Club after 1974 merger
Led the club during its early successful period

figures.name: Rashid Al Dhaheri
figures.title: Racing Driver
figures.roleDescription: Emirati racing driver
figures.significance: He is a notable Emirati racing driver representing the Al Dhaheri family in motorsports.
figures.biography:
Rashid Al Dhaheri is an Emirati racing driver who has represented the United Arab Emirates in motorsports. As a member of the prominent Al Dhaheri family, which traces its roots to the Dhawahir tribe of the Al Ain region, he has carried the family name into the competitive world of motor racing. His involvement in racing represents the modern diversification of prominent Emirati families into various professional fields beyond traditional business and politics.

While specific details about his racing career achievements are not extensively documented in the available sources, his recognition as a notable figure from the Al Dhaheri family indicates his significance within the UAE's motorsports community. The UAE has developed a strong motorsports culture, particularly in Formula racing and rally driving, with several Emirati drivers gaining recognition both regionally and internationally.

figures.name: Mansoor Al Yabhouni Al Dhaheri
figures.title: Film Producer and Entrepreneur
figures.roleDescription: Founder and CEO of Film Gate Productions and Al Kalema Productions
figures.significance: He is a prominent Emirati film producer who has contributed significantly to the development of the UAE film industry and received international recognition for his work.
figures.biography:
Mansoor Al Yabhouni Al Dhaheri was born on 14 January 1975 in Abu Dhabi, United Arab Emirates. He pursued higher education in the United States, earning a Master of Business Administration (MBA) from the New York Institute of Technology (2007–2009) and a Master of Fine Arts (MFA) in Filmmaking from the New York Film Academy (2010–2012). He also received a certificate in Advanced Project Management from Stanford University, demonstrating his commitment to professional development in both business and creative fields.

Al Dhaheri launched Al Kalema Production in January 2012 and Film Gate Production in January 2018, establishing himself as a significant figure in the UAE's growing film industry. His companies have produced a variety of films, TV series, and documentaries that reflect Emirati culture, with productions appearing at prestigious international festivals including the Cannes Film Festival, Dubai International Film Festival, and Abu Dhabi Film Festival. His notable works include 'The Platform' (2020–2021), 'Dreams Drawn by Dust' (2022), 'Scattered Barriers' (2021), 'House of Life' (2022), 'Swim 62' (2023), and 'Abu Dhabi Success Story' (2024). His achievements have been recognized at numerous international festivals, including winning first prize for Best Documentary Film at the Gulf Film Festival in Riyadh (2024) for 'Swim 62', and multiple awards at the New York Film Awards.
achievements: Founded Al Kalema Production (2012) and Film Gate Production (2018)
Won Best Documentary Film at Gulf Film Festival for 'Swim 62' (2024)
Multiple New York Film Awards winner
Productions featured at Cannes Film Festival

figures.name: Ali Saeed Bin Harmal Al Dhaheri
figures.title: Businessman and Former Government Official
figures.roleDescription: Emirati businessman and former government official
figures.significance: He is a notable Emirati businessman and former government official representing the Al Dhaheri family in business and public service.
figures.biography:
Ali Saeed Bin Harmal Al Dhaheri is an accomplished Emirati businessman and former government official who has made significant contributions to both the private and public sectors in the United Arab Emirates. As a member of the prominent Al Dhaheri family, which has historical ties to the Dhawahir tribe of the Al Ain region, he represents the modern generation of the family that has transitioned into business and government roles.

His career spans both government service and private enterprise, reflecting the typical trajectory of many prominent Emirati families who have leveraged their traditional influence into modern economic and political roles. His dual experience in government and business positions him as a bridge between the public and private sectors, contributing to the UAE's economic development and diversification efforts. The Al Dhaheri family's continued prominence in various sectors demonstrates their successful adaptation to the modern UAE's rapidly evolving economy.

figures.name: Jouan Salem Al Dhaheri
figures.title: Politician and ADNOC Executive
figures.roleDescription: Secretary-General and Member of Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (ADNOC)
figures.significance: He serves as Secretary-General and Member of ADNOC, representing the Al Dhaheri family in the UAE's strategic oil and gas sector.
figures.biography:
Jouan Salem Al Dhaheri is a prominent Emirati politician and energy sector executive who currently serves as Secretary-General and Member of Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (ADNOC), one of the world's leading oil and gas companies and a cornerstone of the UAE's economy. His position at ADNOC places him at the heart of the UAE's energy strategy and economic planning, as the company plays a crucial role in the country's oil production, refining, and petrochemicals industries.

As a member of the Al Dhaheri family, which has historical roots in the Al Ain region through the Dhawahir tribe, Jouan represents the family's continued influence in modern UAE governance and strategic industries. His role in ADNOC is particularly significant given the company's importance to the UAE's economy and its position as a major player in global energy markets. His involvement in both political and corporate spheres reflects the integrated approach that characterizes leadership in the UAE, where government and business interests often align in pursuit of national development goals.
achievements: Secretary-General and Member of ADNOC
Listed among notable Federal Government Officials

figures.name: Nasser al-Dhaheri
figures.title: Writer and Journalist
figures.roleDescription: Emirati writer and journalist
figures.significance: He is a notable Emirati writer and journalist representing the Al Dhaheri family in media and literature.
figures.biography:
Nasser al-Dhaheri is an accomplished Emirati writer and journalist who has contributed to the UAE's literary and media landscape. As a member of the Al Dhaheri family, which traces its heritage to the Dhawahir tribe of the Al Ain region, he represents the intellectual and cultural contributions of the family to modern Emirati society. His work in journalism and writing reflects the growing importance of media and literature in the UAE's cultural development.

His career in journalism positions him as a voice in the UAE's media landscape, contributing to public discourse and cultural expression. The recognition of his work among notable figures from the Al Dhaheri family indicates his significance within the UAE's intellectual community. His contributions to literature and journalism represent the diversification of prominent Emirati families into creative and intellectual fields, moving beyond traditional roles in business and politics.

figures.name: Rashid Al Dhaheri
figures.title: Formula 4 Racing Driver
figures.roleDescription: Emirati Formula 4 racing driver
figures.significance: He competed in the 2023 Macau Formula 4 Race, finishing third and representing the UAE in international motorsports.
figures.biography:
Rashid Al Dhaheri is an Emirati Formula 4 racing driver who gained recognition by competing in the prestigious 2023 Macau Formula 4 Race held on 12 November 2023. The race was part of the 2023 Macau Grand Prix and served as an invitational, non-championship round of the 2023 Formula 4 South East Asia Championship. The event featured 23 drivers from 11 teams competing on the challenging Guia Circuit in Macau, one of the most demanding street circuits in motorsports.

Al Dhaheri demonstrated exceptional skill and consistency throughout the weekend's events, which included practice sessions, qualifying, a qualifying race, and the main race. Despite challenging weather conditions that included rain and winds in the morning, making the track slippery with standing water, he managed to secure a podium position. In the final 12-lap race, he finished third behind race winner Arvid Lindblad and second-place finisher Charles Leong. His achievement in finishing on the podium at such a prestigious international racing event represents a significant accomplishment for Emirati motorsports and demonstrates the Al Dhaheri family's presence in competitive racing at the international level.
achievements: Third place finish at 2023 Macau Formula 4 Race
Represented UAE in international Formula 4 competition

figures.name: Saeed Khalifa Humeid Al Rumaithi
figures.title: Former Secretary of the Ministry of Labor in Abu Dhabi
figures.roleDescription: Government official in Abu Dhabi's Ministry of Labor
figures.significance: Served as former secretary of the Ministry of Labor in Abu Dhabi, representing the Al Rumaithi family in government service.
figures.biography:
Saeed Khalifa Humeid Al Rumaithi served as the former secretary of the Ministry of Labor in Abu Dhabi, representing one of the prominent merchant families in the UAE government structure. His role placed him in an important administrative position within the emirate's labor ministry, contributing to the development of labor policies and administration in Abu Dhabi.

As a member of the Al Rumaithi family, his government service demonstrates the family's involvement in public administration and their contribution to the UAE's institutional development during the nation's formative years.
achievements: Served as secretary of the Ministry of Labor in Abu Dhabi

figures.name: Mohammed Khalfan Al Rumaithi
figures.title: Major General, Former Commander of Abu Dhabi Police
figures.roleDescription: Former Commander-in-Chief of Abu Dhabi Police Force
figures.significance: Served as Major General and Commander-in-Chief of Abu Dhabi Police Force from 2016 to 2019, leading one of the UAE's most important security institutions.
figures.biography:
Major General Mohammed Khalfan Al Rumaithi served as the Commander-in-Chief of Abu Dhabi Police Force from 2016 to 2019, during a critical period of modernization and development for the force. His tenure came during the modernization, qualitative and strategic development stage of Abu Dhabi Police, which began in 1995 under Sheikh Saif bin Zayed Al Nahyan's leadership.

As a member of the Al Rumaithi family, his appointment to this senior security position demonstrates the family's trust and standing within UAE security establishments. His leadership of Abu Dhabi Police placed him in charge of one of the most technologically advanced police forces in the region, overseeing approximately 36,000 total staff including civil defence, ambulance and fire services and border security.
achievements: Served as Commander-in-Chief of Abu Dhabi Police Force (2016-2019)
Led major security institution during modernization period

figures.name: Thani Obeid Khamees Al Rumaithi
figures.title: Brigadier, Former Commander of Abu Dhabi Police
figures.roleDescription: Former Commander of Abu Dhabi Police Force
figures.significance: Served as Brigadier and Commander of Abu Dhabi Police from 1991 to 1995, contributing to the force's development during the progressive stage.
figures.biography:
Brigadier Thani Obeid Khamees Al Rumaithi served as Commander of Abu Dhabi Police from 1991 to 1995, during what is known as the progressive stage of the force's development. His tenure came during a crucial period when Abu Dhabi Police was evolving toward eventual affiliation and merging into the Federal Ministry of Interior, following the 1979 merger that made the force part of the federal police and security forces.

His leadership bridged an important transitional period for Abu Dhabi Police, serving just before Sheikh Saif bin Zayed Al Nahyan assumed the role of General Commander in 1995, which marked the beginning of the modernization and strategic development phase of the force.
achievements: Served as Commander of Abu Dhabi Police (1991-1995)
Led force during progressive development stage

figures.name: Ali Mohammed Bakheet Al Rumaithi
figures.title: Board Member, Central Bank of the UAE
figures.roleDescription: Member of the Board of Directors of the Central Bank of the UAE
figures.significance: Serves as a board member of the Central Bank of the UAE, representing the Al Rumaithi family in the country's highest financial institution.
figures.biography:
Ali Mohammed Bakheet Al Rumaithi serves as a member of the Board of Directors of the Central Bank of the United Arab Emirates (CBUAE), the state institution responsible for managing the country's currency, monetary policy, banking and insurance regulation. His appointment to this prestigious position places him among the key decision-makers for the UAE's financial and monetary policies.

As a board member of the CBUAE, Al Rumaithi participates in overseeing the institution's mandate to issue and manage the UAE dirham, ensure currency stability, manage the UAE's credit policy, develop and oversee the banking system, and represent the UAE in international financial institutions such as the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank. His role demonstrates the Al Rumaithi family's involvement in the UAE's financial sector governance.
achievements: Board member of the Central Bank of the UAE
Involved in UAE monetary policy governance

figures.name: Mohammed bin Musallam bin Ham al-Ameri
figures.title: Sheikh
figures.roleDescription: Emirati billionaire businessman, author and former UAE Federal National Council member
figures.significance: Emirati billionaire who pledged half his wealth to charity and served as Deputy Chairman of the Bin Ham Group.
figures.biography:
Sheikh Mohammed Bin Musallam Bin Ham Al-Ameri was born on January 11, 1972, in Al Ain, a city of the Emirate of Abu Dhabi, descended from a family known for its interest in public service.
